anti-pll.description: Anti PLL - 22 algorithms for recognizing and solving PLL cases from the inverse angle. Used for advanced last layer prediction.

cll.description: Corners of the Last Layer for 2x2 - 42 cases across 7 subsets. Solves the last layer corners in one step after completing the first layer.

cmll.description: Corners of the Last Layer for Roux method - 42 cases. Solves corners while preserving the M-slice and oriented edges. Used in the Roux method.

coll.description: Corners of the Last Layer - 40 cases. These algorithms orient and permute corners while preserving edge orientation, leaving only an EPLL to solve.

csp.description: Cubeshape Parity algorithms for Square-1 - 20 cases. These algorithms fix parity during or after the cubeshape step, ensuring the puzzle can be solved without encountering parity errors later.

eg-2.description: EG-2 algorithms for 2x2 - 40 cases. Used when the first layer face is solved but the D-layer corners have a diagonal swap. Advanced 2x2 method.

eg1.description: EG-1 algorithms for 2x2 - 42 cases. Used when the first layer face is solved but the D-layer corners are not permuted (adjacent swap). Advanced 2x2 method.

ell.description: Edges of the Last Layer - 25 algorithms to solve the last layer edges when corners are already solved. Used in some ZZ and Roux variants.

f2l.description: First Two Layers - 42 intuitive and algorithmic cases for simultaneously solving the first two layers. The second step of the CFOP method.

l3e.description: Last 3 Edges - 5 algorithms for solving the last 3 edges of the Pyraminx. Essential for sub-5 solves.

l4e.description: Last 4 Edges - solve the final 4 edges of the Pyraminx. 16 cases. After solving all corners and placing one edge, these algorithms solve the remaining four edges.

master-pyraminx.description: Algorithms for the Master Pyraminx (4-layer Pyraminx). Layer-by-layer approach with inner layer and edge algorithms.

mega-co.description: Megaminx Corner Orientation - 16 algorithms for orienting corners on the last layer of the Megaminx.

mega-cp.description: Megaminx Corner Permutation - 15 algorithms for permuting corners on the last layer of the Megaminx.

mega-eo.description: Megaminx Edge Orientation - 3 algorithms for orienting edges on the last layer of the Megaminx.

mega-ep.description: Megaminx Edge Permutation - 5 algorithms for permuting edges on the last layer of the Megaminx.

mega-oll.description: Megaminx OLL - Full set of Orientation of the Last Layer algorithms for Megaminx, covering all edge and corner orientation cases.

mega-pll.description: Megaminx PLL - Full set of Permutation of the Last Layer algorithms for Megaminx.

oll-2x2.description: Ortega OLL for 2x2 — 7 cases. Orients all pieces on the last layer of the 2x2 cube. Part of the Ortega method.

oll-parity.description: OLL Parity algorithms for 4x4 — 12 cases. Fixes the parity error where a single edge is flipped on the last layer, which is impossible on a 3x3.

oll.description: Orientation of the Last Layer - 57 cases. These algorithms orient all last layer pieces so the top face is one color. Part of the CFOP method.

ollcp.description: OLLCP (OLL + Corner Permutation) — orientation of the last layer plus corner permutation in one step. Advanced extension of OLL with ~342 cases across 57 OLL shapes.

pbl.description: Permutation of Both Layers — 6 cases. Solves both layers simultaneously on the 2x2 cube after OLL. Part of the Ortega method.

pll-parity.description: PLL Parity algorithms for 4x4 — 22 cases. Fixes parity errors during the PLL step where two edges or corners need to be swapped in a way impossible on a 3x3.

pll.description: Permutation of the Last Layer - 21 cases. These algorithms permute all last layer pieces to their correct positions. The final step of the CFOP method.

pyra-bl5e.description: Bheem Last 5 Edges for Pyraminx. An alternative L5E method with R and L variants.

pyra-kl5e.description: Krish Last 5 Edges for Pyraminx. An advanced L5E recognition and solving method with separate R and L variants.

pyra-l5e-bad.description: Last 5 Edges (Bad Layers) for Pyraminx. Advanced L5E cases with Nutella, Peanut, Krish, and 2-Flip subsets.

pyra-l5e-ht.description: Last 5 Edges Heads/Tails for Pyraminx. L5E cases categorized by Heads and Tails patterns.

pyra-l5e-yy.description: Last 5 Edges Yin/Yang for Pyraminx. L5E cases categorized by Yin and Yang patterns.

pyra-ml4e.description: Modified Last 4 Edges for Pyraminx. An optimized variant of L4E with Right Slot and Left Slot approaches.

pyra-tl4e-b.description: Twisted Last 4 Edges (Bottom) for Pyraminx. L4E cases with a twisted bottom tip, covering both + and - orientations.

pyra-tl4e-r.description: Twisted Last 4 Edges (Right) for Pyraminx. L4E cases with a twisted right tip, covering bar positions and both orientations.

pyra-top-first.description: Top First method for Pyraminx. Solves the top tip first with various techniques including 1-Flip, Nutella, WO, Oka, Bell, and more.

sarah.description: Sarah's intermediate method for Skewb - 11 cases for the last layer. After solving the first layer (one face plus adjacent centers), these algorithms complete the puzzle.

sarahs-advanced.description: Sarah's Advanced method for Skewb - 81 algorithms covering all Pi and Peanut cases with various permutations.

sbls.description: Second Block Last Slot - 65 algorithms for inserting the last F2L pair while solving the second block in Roux method.

sq1-co.description: Corner Orientation algorithms for Square-1 - 7 cases. These algorithms orient the corners after cubeshape is solved.

sq1-cp.description: Corner Permutation algorithms for Square-1 - 8 cases. These algorithms permute the corners after orientation is complete.

sq1-cs.description: Square-1 Cube Shape - 112 algorithms for transforming the Square-1 from scrambled shapes back into cube shape.

sq1-eo.description: Edge Orientation algorithms for Square-1 - 7 cases. These algorithms orient the edges after corners are oriented.

sq1-ep.description: Square-1 Edge Permutation - 49 algorithms for permuting edges after orientation and corner permutation.

sq1-parity.description: Square-1 Parity algorithms - 5 cases for fixing parity on the Square-1 puzzle.

sv.description: Summer Variation — 27 cases. Orients the last layer while inserting the final F2L pair when the pair is connected with corner on bottom. Skips OLL entirely.

vls.description: Valk Last Slot — solves the last F2L pair while orienting the last layer edges. Large advanced algorithm set with cases split across different edge orientations.

wv.description: Winter Variation — 27 cases. Orients the last layer while inserting the final F2L pair when the pair is connected with corner on top. Skips OLL entirely.

zbll-as.description: ZBLL Anti-Sune subset - 72 cases. Solves the last layer when edges are oriented and the corner pattern matches the Anti-Sune shape.

zbll-h.description: ZBLL H subset - 40 cases. Solves the last layer when edges are oriented and the corner pattern matches the H permutation.

zbll-l.description: ZBLL L subset - 70 cases. Solves the last layer when edges are oriented and the corner pattern matches the L shape.

zbll-pi.description: ZBLL Pi subset - 67 cases. Solves the last layer when edges are oriented and the corner pattern matches the Pi shape.

zbll-s.description: ZBLL S subset - 72 cases. Solves the last layer when edges are oriented and the corner pattern matches the Sune shape.

zbll-t.description: ZBLL T subset - 67 cases. Solves the last layer when edges are oriented and the corner pattern matches the T permutation.

zbll-u.description: ZBLL U subset - 67 cases. Solves the last layer when edges are oriented and the corner pattern matches the U permutation. Part of the full ZBLL algorithm set.

2-look-oll.description: Beginner-friendly OLL in 2 steps: first orient edges (make a cross), then orient corners. Only 9 algorithms instead of the full 57 OLL cases.

2-look-pll.description: Beginner-friendly PLL in 2 steps: first permute corners (2 cases), then permute edges (4 cases). Only 6 algorithms instead of the full 21 PLL cases.

epll.description: Edge Permutation of the Last Layer - 4 cases. Used in ZZ, Petrus, and other EO-first methods where edges are already oriented after OLL.

beginner-last-layer.description: The simplest last layer method for absolute beginners. 4 intuitive steps: orient edges, orient corners, permute corners, permute edges.

vhls.description: Vandenbergh-Harris Last Slot - 32 cases. Advanced F2L technique that orients last layer edges while inserting the last F2L pair.

leg-1.description: Left EG-1 - Solves the last layer when a 1x1x2 bar is held on the left side. An EG-1 variant providing different algorithm angles for advanced 2x2 solving.

tcll-plus.description: Twisty CLL+ - CLL cases where one corner is twisted clockwise. Solves both the CLL case and the corner twist simultaneously.

tcll-minus.description: Twisty CLL- - CLL cases where one corner is twisted counter-clockwise. Mirrors TCLL+ for the opposite twist direction.

ls-1.description: Last Slot 1 - One-look last-slot algorithms for 2x2. Solves the last slot while orienting and permuting the remaining corners in one step.

ls-2.description: Last Slot 2 - One-look last-slot algorithms for 2x2. Solves the last slot while orienting and permuting the remaining corners in one step.

ls-3.description: Last Slot 3 - One-look last-slot algorithms for 2x2. Solves the last slot while orienting and permuting the remaining corners in one step.

ls-4.description: Last Slot 4 - One-look last-slot algorithms for 2x2. Solves the last slot while orienting and permuting the remaining corners in one step.

ls-5.description: Last Slot 5 - One-look last-slot algorithms for 2x2. Solves the last slot while orienting and permuting the remaining corners in one step.

ls-6.description: Last Slot 6 - One-look last-slot algorithms for 2x2. Solves the last slot while orienting and permuting the remaining corners in one step.

ls-7.description: Last Slot 7 - One-look last-slot algorithms for 2x2. Solves the last slot while orienting and permuting the remaining corners in one step.

ls-8.description: Last Slot 8 - One-look last-slot algorithms for 2x2. Solves the last slot while orienting and permuting the remaining corners in one step.

ls-9.description: Last Slot 9 - One-look last-slot algorithms for 2x2. Solves the last slot while orienting and permuting the remaining corners in one step.

8355.description: A 3x3 method created by Gilles Roux (the creator of the Roux method) that breaks the solve into four distinct phases based on the number of pieces solved in each step: 8 pieces, then 3 more, then 5 more, then the final 5. It is a corners-first variant that solves all corners first, then tackles edges in groups. The method is notable for its unconventional approach and low algorithm count.

steps.description: Solve all 8 corners of the cube. This can be done using a corners-first approach — first solve the 4 bottom corners, then orient and permute the 4 top corners. With practice, all 8 corners can be solved intuitively or with minimal algorithms.
Insert 3 of the 4 middle-layer edges into their correct positions. Leave one middle-layer slot empty to use as a working space for subsequent steps.

Solve the 4 top-layer edges and the remaining middle-layer edge simultaneously. This step uses the empty middle slot to manipulate edges into place. Typically involves orienting and then permuting the edges.
Solve the final 4 bottom-layer edges plus any remaining edge to complete the cube. This typically involves flipping the cube and using edge-cycling techniques.

pros: Very few algorithms required
Unique corners-first approach builds understanding of the cube
Created by Gilles Roux, a respected method designer
Good for developing intuitive solving skills
cons: Higher move count than Roux or CFOP
Not competitive for speedcubing
Limited resources and community
Edge steps can be tricky for beginners

3-style.description: The most advanced and fastest method for 3x3 blindfolded solving. 3-Style uses commutator-based 3-cycles (hence the name '3-style') to solve three pieces at a time rather than swapping two pieces. Each algorithm is a commutator of the form [A, B] = A B A' B', which cycles three pieces while leaving everything else untouched. With unique algorithms for every possible 3-cycle of edges and corners, 3-style requires learning hundreds of algorithms but produces drastically lower move counts and faster execution than Old Pochmann or M2. All world-class 3BLD solvers use 3-style.

steps.description: Memorize edge cycles using letter tracing with a buffer piece (commonly UF or DF). The memo generates pairs of letters — each pair represents one 3-cycle to execute. Unlike Old Pochmann where each letter is one swap, in 3-style each pair of letters is one commutator that cycles three edges.

Memorize corner cycles using letter tracing with a buffer piece (commonly UBL or UFR). Like edges, each pair of letters represents one 3-cycle commutator. Corner memo is typically done using visual/image memo with letter pairs.

Execute corner 3-cycles using commutator algorithms. Each commutator [A, B] cycles the buffer piece and two target pieces. There are hundreds of unique corner commutators depending on the target positions. Commutators typically consist of an interchange move, an insertion move, and their inverses, totaling 8-12 moves per cycle.

Execute edge 3-cycles using commutator algorithms. Edge commutators often use M-slice and S-slice moves for efficiency. Like corners, each commutator cycles three edges (buffer + two targets) in 8-12 moves.

pros: Fastest possible 3BLD execution method
Very low move count (40-80 total moves for execution)
Used by all world-class 3BLD solvers
Sub-15 single solves achievable by elite solvers
Elegant mathematical foundation in commutator theory
Applicable to bigger cube BLD as well
cons: Hundreds of unique commutator algorithms to learn
Extremely steep learning curve
Significant time investment to become proficient
Deriving commutators on the fly is a skill that takes months to develop
Recognition and recall speed are challenging

alyz.description: The Alyz method is an intermediate Clock solving technique. It structures the solve into three front steps, a flip, three back steps, and four simultaneous corner moves. This balanced approach offers more optimization than beginner flip methods while being more accessible than the expert-level 7-Simul technique.

steps.description: Solve the first set of clocks on the front face using specific pin configurations and wheel turns. This begins the structured front-face solution.
Continue solving the front face by correcting additional clocks. Build on the progress from step 1 without disturbing already-solved positions.
Complete the front-face work by solving the remaining front clocks. After this step, the front face should be in a favorable state for the flip.
Flip the Clock puzzle over to access the back face. The front face work is complete at this point.
Begin solving the back face by correcting clocks using appropriate pin and wheel combinations.
Continue solving the back face, building on the progress from back step 1.
Complete the back-face edge clocks, setting up for the final corner moves.

Solve all four corners simultaneously with pre-planned moves. Since all edge clocks are solved, the corners can be corrected with specific pin-and-wheel combinations that affect only corner positions.

pros: More structured than beginner methods
Simultaneous corner finish is efficient
Good balance between speed and learning difficulty
Clear progression path from beginner to advanced Clock solving
cons: Still requires a flip, unlike 7-Simul
More steps than minimal methods
Front/back interaction requires careful planning
Not fast enough for world-record-level solves

apb.description: APB (Athefre's Pair and Block) is a modern method that combines blockbuilding with strategic pair placement and edge orientation. It builds a 2x2x3 block, places a down-back-right pair, orients edges while inserting another pair, then finishes with LXS (Last X Slots) techniques. APB is part of the newer wave of method development that seeks efficient alternatives to traditional CFOP and Roux approaches.

steps.description: Build a 2x2x3 block on the left side of the cube. This block consists of the entire left layer (1x2x3) plus an additional corner-edge pair extending it to a 2x2x3 shape.

Place the down-back-right (dBR) corner-edge pair into its correct position. This pair fills a specific slot in the first two layers and sets up the solve for efficient EO + pair insertion in the next step.
Orient all remaining edges while simultaneously inserting the last remaining F2L pair. This step combines two objectives to save moves and sets up the cube for a smooth last layer finish.

Solve the remaining pieces on the last layer. With edges oriented and F2L complete, the last layer is reduced, and various algorithm sets can be applied depending on the approach chosen (ZBLL, COLL+EPLL, or OCLL+PLL).

pros: Low move count (~42-50 moves)
Efficient EO integration saves moves
Flexible last layer options
Modern design with growing community interest
Good balance of intuition and algorithms
cons: EOPair step recognition is complex
2x2x3 block building requires strong planning
Limited tutorials and resources
Small community of practitioners
Requires good understanding of edge orientation

balint.description: The Balint method is an advanced Megaminx speedsolving technique created by Balint Bodor. It follows a structured approach of solving the star (first layer edges), F2L pairs, freestyle S2L (Second-to-Last Layer), and finishing with the last layer. The freestyle S2L phase distinguishes it from other methods by allowing more intuitive and flexible solving of the penultimate layers.

steps.description: Solve the first layer edges to form a star pattern on the bottom face of the Megaminx. This establishes the foundation for the F2L phase.
Solve the first two layers by pairing corners with their adjacent edges and inserting them into their correct slots. This is similar to 3x3 F2L but extended to the Megaminx's pentagonal geometry.
Solve the second-to-last layer using freestyle techniques. Unlike rigid algorithmic approaches, this step allows creative block building and intuitive piece placement to handle the S2L efficiently.
Solve the last layer of the Megaminx using OLL and PLL algorithms adapted for the Megaminx. This includes orienting and then permuting all last-layer pieces.

pros: Freestyle S2L allows creative and flexible solving
Lower move count potential than fully algorithmic S2L approaches
Adapts well to different scrambles
Good for solvers with strong intuitive solving skills
cons: Freestyle S2L has a steep learning curve
Inconsistent S2L times due to variable difficulty
Requires deep understanding of Megaminx piece interactions
Last layer algorithms are lengthy and require significant memorization

beginner.description: The layer-by-layer method most people learn first. Solves the cube one layer at a time with a small set of easy-to-memorize algorithms.

steps.description: Form a plus sign on the white face, ensuring edge pieces match adjacent center colors.
Insert the 4 white corner pieces to complete the first layer.
Insert the 4 middle layer edge pieces using two mirror algorithms.
Form a yellow cross on the top face (orientation only).
Orient all yellow corners so the entire top face is yellow.
Move yellow corners to their correct positions (ignoring orientation which is already done).
Cycle the last layer edges into their correct positions to complete the solve.

pros: Easy to learn and memorize
Very few algorithms (5-7)
Great foundation for CFOP
Abundant tutorials available
cons: Very high move count (~120+ moves)
Slow solve times
Not efficient for speed
Layer restriction limits optimization

bell.description: An advanced Pyraminx method created by Drew Brads. The Bell method is designed for top-level Pyraminx speedcubing and focuses on placing one edge to form a V-shape, then solving the remaining edges using optimized one-look algorithms. What distinguishes Bell from standard L4E is its emphasis on influencing edge orientation during the V-building step and its optimized algorithm set for the remaining edges, allowing for shorter and more ergonomic solutions.

steps.description: Twist all four trivial tips to match their adjacent center colors. Done at any point during the solve.

Solve one edge to form a V-shape at the back of the Pyraminx while simultaneously influencing the orientation of the remaining edges. By choosing specific move sequences to build the V, you can set up the remaining edges into favorable L4E cases. This is the key innovation of the Bell method.

Solve the remaining four edges in one look using an optimized Bell algorithm set. Because the V was built with edge influence, many of the harder L4E cases are avoided, and the remaining cases have been optimized for speed and ergonomics.

pros: One of the fastest Pyraminx methods available
Edge influence during V reduces hard L4E cases
Optimized algorithms for ergonomic speed
Very low move count
Used by top Pyraminx competitors
cons: Requires deep understanding of edge influence
Inspection planning is more demanding than standard L4E
Fewer resources than standard L4E
Marginal improvement over L4E may not justify the added complexity for non-elite solvers

belt.description: An unconventional method that begins by solving the four middle-layer (equatorial) edges first, forming a 'belt' or ring around the cube's equator. After the belt is formed, the top and bottom layers are solved. This approach inverts the typical layer-by-layer logic and offers a fresh perspective on the cube, though it is generally less efficient than mainstream methods.

steps.description: Solve the four edges of the middle layer (E-slice), forming a complete ring or belt around the equator of the cube. Each edge must be placed in its correct position with correct orientation.
Solve all corners and edges of one face (top or bottom) while preserving the belt. This involves placing four edges and four corners into their correct positions.
Solve the four edges of the remaining face. Since the belt and one full face are solved, these edges must be placed carefully to avoid disruption.
Orient and permute the final four corners to complete the solve. This is the last step and can use OLL/PLL corner subsets or dedicated algorithms.

pros: Unique and interesting approach to the cube
Good for developing spatial awareness
Middle layer is solved early, providing a visual anchor
Relatively easy to understand conceptually
cons: High move count (~60-80 moves)
Not competitive for speedsolving
Limited resources and community support
Later steps are constrained by the need to preserve the belt
Less ergonomic than layer or block methods

bencisco.description: The Bencisco method is an intermediate FTO (Face-Turning Octahedron) solving technique created by Ben Streeter in 2018. It structures the FTO solve into block building, center solving, and corner-center pair insertion. This method provides a clear and logical framework for tackling the FTO puzzle efficiently.

steps.description: Build an initial block on the FTO by combining connected pieces into a solved section. This block serves as the anchor for the rest of the solve and is built intuitively.
Solve the remaining center pieces of the FTO without disturbing the built block. Centers are placed using short algorithmic sequences or intuitive moves.

Solve the remaining pieces by pairing corners with their adjacent centers and inserting them into their correct positions. This is similar in concept to F2L on a 3x3 but adapted to the FTO's octahedral geometry.

pros: Logical and structured approach to the FTO
Block building provides a strong foundation
Accessible for solvers transitioning from 3x3 methods
Good balance of intuition and algorithmic solving
cons: FTO-specific techniques require dedicated practice
Block building efficiency varies significantly between solves
Limited community resources and tutorials
FTO geometry is unfamiliar to most cubers initially

bh.description: The BH (Beyer-Hardwick) method is an expert-level 3BLD (3x3 blindfolded) solving technique. It uses pre-memorized 3-cycle commutators for both edges and corners, allowing the solver to solve any piece cycle in a single optimized algorithm. This is the gold standard for competitive BLD solving and is used by virtually all top BLD solvers worldwide.

steps.description: Memorize the complete cycle sequence for both edges and corners. Advanced memory techniques such as letter pairs, images, and memory palaces are used to encode the solving sequence rapidly.

Solve all edges using pre-memorized 3-cycle commutators. Each pair of letters in the memo corresponds to one commutator that cycles three edge pieces. Every possible edge target combination has a specific optimized commutator.
Solve all corners using pre-memorized 3-cycle commutators. Similar to edges, each memo pair triggers a specific corner commutator that cycles three corner pieces.

pros: Fastest BLD execution method available
Used by all top-level BLD solvers
Optimal move count for each 3-cycle
Scales well to multi-blind and bigger cubes
cons: Requires memorizing hundreds of commutators
Extremely steep learning curve
Takes months or years to fully learn all cases
Deriving forgotten commutators mid-solve is slow

briggs.description: The Briggs method is a CP-first Roux variant created by Joseph Briggs in 2015. It starts with CPFB (corner permutation + first block), builds a 1x2x2 square on the right side, then inserts a pair while simultaneously orienting corners and creating an arrow on the last layer. The final step is a reduced last layer that benefits from pre-solved corner permutation. Briggs was one of the earlier methods to explore the CP-first concept that has since gained traction in the method development community.

steps.description: Solve corner permutation for all corners while simultaneously building a 1x2x3 first block on the left side. This is the most planning-intensive step and determines the efficiency of the rest of the solve.
Build a 1x2x2 block (one corner-edge pair plus one edge) on the right side of the cube. This partially fills the second block and sets up the next step.

Insert the last right-block pair while simultaneously orienting the remaining corners and creating an 'arrow' pattern on the last layer (one correctly oriented edge adjacent to the solving slot). This multi-purpose step is the key innovation of the Briggs method.

Solve the remaining last layer pieces. With corner permutation and orientation already solved and an arrow established, the last layer is reduced to a small set of edge-only cases, solvable with M and U moves.

pros: Reduced last layer due to CP-first approach
Low move count (~42-50 moves)
Innovative multi-purpose steps
Good ergonomics with RU and MU movesets
Pioneering CP-first design that influenced later methods
cons: CPFB planning is very demanding
Multi-purpose steps have complex recognition
Small community and limited resources
Pair + CO + Arrow step has many cases
Steep learning curve for CP recognition

ceor.description: CEOR is a modern method that begins by permuting corners and building a first block simultaneously (CPFB), followed by edge orientation with the dM slice, then blockbuilding to complete the first two layers, and finishing with a reduced last layer. By solving corner permutation early, CEOR reduces the last layer to a smaller algorithm set. It represents the growing trend of CP-first methods in modern speedcubing theory.

steps.description: Simultaneously permute all corners into their correct orbits and build a 1x2x3 first block on the left side. Corner permutation is solved relative to the first block, ensuring that CP is resolved for the rest of the solve.
Orient all remaining edges while placing edges into the dM slice (the down and middle layer columns on the right side). This step prepares the cube for efficient right-block construction.
Complete the right-side 1x2x3 block to finish the first two layers. Since edges are oriented and corner permutation is solved, this step is straightforward blockbuilding.

Solve the last layer with a reduced algorithm set. Since corner permutation and edge orientation are already solved, the last layer only requires corner orientation and edge permutation, significantly fewer cases than full OLL+PLL.

pros: Reduced last layer due to early CP solving
Low move count (~42-50 moves)
Ergonomic RU-dominant moveset
No cube rotations after first block
Strong theoretical efficiency
cons: CPFB planning is extremely difficult
CP recognition takes significant practice
Very limited resources and tutorials
Small community of practitioners
Steep learning curve for the CP-first concept

cfop.description: The most popular speedcubing method, used by the majority of world-class solvers. CFOP stands for Cross, F2L, OLL, PLL — four distinct stages that break the solve into manageable pieces.

steps.description: Solve the 4 edge pieces of one face (usually white) to form a plus sign on the bottom layer, ensuring edges align with center colors.
Pair corner and edge pieces together and insert them into their correct slots, solving the first two layers simultaneously.
Orient all pieces on the last layer so the top face is one solid color (yellow if white cross).
Permute all last layer pieces to their correct positions, completing the solve.

pros: Most resources and tutorials available
Efficient move count (~55-60 moves)
Clear progression path
Best for speed once mastered
cons: 57 OLL + 21 PLL algorithms to learn
F2L can be difficult to learn intuitively
Heavy algorithm dependence

cll-method.description: A one-look last layer method for the 2x2 cube. After solving one face with correct permutation (a solved layer), a single CLL algorithm solves the remaining four corners in one step. CLL contains 42 algorithms and is the standard advanced method for 2x2, bridging the gap between Ortega and the more demanding EG method. It is widely used by competitive 2x2 solvers.

steps.description: Solve one complete layer of the 2x2 — all four pieces must be correctly positioned and oriented relative to each other (not just a face). This means the bottom face is solved AND the four bottom side stickers are correct. This is harder than Ortega's first step since permutation matters.

Solve all four remaining corners in a single algorithm. CLL simultaneously orients and permutes the last layer corners. There are 42 algorithms divided into categories: Sune (6), Antisune (6), H (4), Pi (6), T (6), U (6), L (6), and solved/skip (2). Recognition is based on the pattern of top-face stickers and the side sticker arrangements.

pros: One-look last layer is very fast
42 algorithms is manageable for dedicated cubers
Well-documented with many resources available
Natural progression from Ortega
Sub-2 times are achievable with mastery
cons: First layer must be fully solved (permuted), making it slower than EG's face approach
42 algorithms require dedicated learning time
Not as fast as EG at the highest level
First layer planning in inspection is more demanding than Ortega

columns-first.description: A lesser-known 3x3 method that solves the cube by first building columns (corner-edge pairs) without regard to their relative positions, then uses algorithms to solve the remaining pieces. Instead of building layers, the solver creates four vertical columns connecting opposite faces, then orients and permutes remaining pieces. It is considered a novel approach that provides a different perspective on the cube.

steps.description: Create four columns by pairing each bottom-layer corner with its corresponding top-layer corner via the connecting edge. Each column is a vertical 1x1x3 strip. The four columns are built without necessarily being in the correct positions relative to each other.
Ensure all columns are oriented correctly so that top and bottom face colors face up and down respectively. This is similar to an orientation step but applied to the column structures.
Move all four columns into their correct positions relative to each other. After this step, the top and bottom layers' corners are solved, and the middle layer edges remain.
Insert the four middle-layer edges into their correct positions to complete the solve. Various techniques can be used including commutators or dedicated algorithms.

pros: Unique approach that develops strong understanding of cube structure
Relatively few algorithms needed
Good for developing spatial reasoning
Interesting alternative perspective to layer-based methods
cons: Not competitive for speedcubing
Higher move count than mainstream methods
Very few resources and tutorials available
Column building can be inefficient

domino-reduction.description: Domino Reduction is a two-phase method that first reduces the cube to a 'domino state' where all pieces are oriented and E-slice edges are separated, then solves the cube using only half-turn moves on the restricted axes. It is named because the reduced state resembles the constraints of a domino puzzle. This method is widely used in Fewest Moves Challenge (FMC) as the first phase of many competitive solutions, and its concepts underpin Thistlethwaite's and Kociemba's computer algorithms.

steps.description: Orient all 12 edges so that none require an F or B quarter turn to be solved. Bad edges are identified and flipped using F/B moves, after which F and B are restricted to double turns only.

Orient all 8 corners (so all U/D stickers face U or D) and separate the 4 E-slice edges into the E-slice. The E-slice edges do not need to be permuted correctly yet. After this step, the cube is in the 'domino' state.

Solve the cube completely using only R2, L2, F2, B2, U, and D moves (half turns on R/L/F/B, any turns on U/D). All pieces are in their correct orbits, so the remaining solve is constrained to this reduced move group.

pros: Very low move count (~30-40 moves)
Essential technique for competitive FMC
Clean theoretical framework based on group theory
Domino state is a powerful intermediate goal
Concepts transfer to many other methods
cons: CO + E-slice separation is challenging to plan
Not efficient for speedsolving due to planning overhead
Requires understanding of piece orientation theory
Half-turn phase still requires significant thought
Better suited for FMC than timed solving

eg-method.description: The most advanced and fastest method for 2x2 speedcubing. EG (named after Erik Akkersdijk and Gunnar Krig) builds upon CLL by relaxing the requirement for a fully solved first layer. Instead, the solver only needs to complete a face on the bottom (oriented, not necessarily permuted), then applies a single algorithm to solve the entire remaining cube. EG encompasses three subsets: EG-0 (layer solved, identical to CLL), EG-1 (face solved with adjacent swap on bottom), and EG-2 (face solved with diagonal swap on bottom). Together these total around 130 algorithms.

steps.description: Solve one face of the 2x2 (all four stickers the same color on one side) and place it on the bottom. Unlike CLL, the four corners do NOT need to be correctly permuted. This is the same first step as Ortega. You then identify which EG subset applies: EG-0 if the bottom layer happens to be fully solved, EG-1 if there is an adjacent corner swap, or EG-2 if there is a diagonal corner swap.

Apply a single algorithm to solve the entire cube from the face-solved state. The algorithm set depends on the bottom layer permutation state: EG-0 (same as CLL, 42 algorithms) when the bottom layer is solved, EG-1 (42 algorithms) when there is an adjacent swap in the bottom layer, and EG-2 (42 algorithms) when there is a diagonal swap. Recognition involves reading both the top face pattern and the side stickers to determine the exact case.

pros: Fastest possible 2x2 method — used by all top competitors
First face is easier to solve than CLL's first layer (permutation not required)
One-look solve from face state
Sub-1.5 averages achievable by top solvers
Very flexible first step
cons: Approximately 130 algorithms total (EG-0 + EG-1 + EG-2)
Recognition is complex due to identifying bottom layer state and top pattern simultaneously
Significant time investment to learn all subsets
Diminishing returns over CLL for casual solvers

flip-clock.description: The Flip method is a beginner-friendly Clock solving technique. It solves the front cross first, then the front corners, flips the puzzle, and solves the entire back side. This approach completes the front face fully before moving to the back, providing a straightforward and intuitive solving experience.

steps.description: Solve the four edge clocks on the front face to form a cross pattern. Use appropriate pin configurations to isolate and set each edge clock to 12 o'clock.
Solve the four corner clocks on the front face. Since the front cross is already solved, use pin configurations that adjust corners without disturbing the edges.
Flip the Clock puzzle over to access the back face. The front face should be fully solved at this point.
Solve all clocks on the back face — both edges and corners. Use pin configurations that preserve the solved front face while correcting the back.

pros: Very intuitive — solve one face completely before the other
Easy to learn for Clock beginners
Clear structure with minimal confusion
Good starting method before learning no-flip techniques
cons: Flip adds time to every solve
Back side solving can be constrained by front face preservation
Not used at competitive levels
Limited optimization compared to simultaneous methods

fmc-blockbuilding.description: The foundational approach for Fewest Moves Challenge (FMC) solving. FMC Blockbuilding involves constructing blocks of solved pieces (typically 2x2x2 and 2x2x3 blocks) using efficient move sequences, then extending these blocks to complete the solve in as few moves as possible. Unlike speedcubing methods that prioritize speed, FMC blockbuilding prioritizes move efficiency above all else. Solvers search for lucky cases, use NISS, insertions, and other FMC techniques in combination with blockbuilding. This is the core strategy used by virtually all competitive FMC solvers.

steps.description: Search for an efficient way to build a 2x2x2 block (one corner and three adjacent edges plus their connecting pieces). Try multiple starting moves and orientations to find a block that can be built in very few moves (ideally 3-5). This is a brute-force search through many possible starting sequences.

Extend the 2x2x2 block into a 2x2x3 block by adding one additional edge and one corner-edge pair. This larger block solves a 2x2x3 section of the cube. Search for the most efficient extension, trying different approaches and move sequences.

Solve the first two layers except for one corner-edge slot. This leaves a 'skeleton' that is almost complete. Solving this step efficiently often requires creative blockbuilding, pair insertions, and pseudo-block techniques. The unsolved slot gives flexibility for the next step.

Finish the last F2L slot and solve the last layer. In FMC, this is often done by finding a 'skeleton' — a sequence that solves everything except a few pieces (typically a 3-cycle of corners or edges). Then use insertions to solve the remaining pieces within the existing skeleton, adding minimal extra moves.

pros: Core technique used by all top FMC solvers
Develops deep understanding of cube structure
Can achieve sub-25 move solutions with skill
World record solutions use blockbuilding as the foundation
Flexible and creative process
cons: Requires extensive search and trial-and-error
Not applicable to speedcubing (too slow to think through)
1-hour time limit means search must be efficient
Very steep learning curve for optimal blockbuilding
Results vary widely based on the scramble

fmc-niss.description: A fundamental FMC (Fewest Moves Challenge) technique that allows the solver to work on the scramble and its inverse interchangeably, combining moves from both directions into a single efficient solution. NISS exploits the mathematical property that solving on the inverse scramble is equivalent to prepending moves to the normal solution. By switching between normal and inverse scrambles, solvers can find blocks, pairs, and continuations that would be invisible working from only one direction. NISS is not a complete method by itself but rather a powerful technique used within FMC blockbuilding.

steps.description: Begin by working on the normal scramble. Search for efficient blocks, pairs, or useful structures. Write down the moves you find. If you reach a point where continuation is difficult or inefficient, consider switching to the inverse.

Apply the inverse of your current solution to the inverse scramble, creating a new state to work from. On this new state, continue building blocks or searching for efficient continuations. Moves found on the inverse will become premoves when translated back to the normal solution. The key insight: moves done on the inverse scramble are prepended (in reverse) to the normal solution.

Merge the moves found on normal and inverse scrambles into a single solution. Moves from the inverse scramble (in reverse order) are prepended to the normal solution. Check for cancellations where normal and inverse moves meet — adjacent moves may cancel, saving moves.

After combining NISS sequences, you typically have a 'skeleton' that solves most of the cube except for a few pieces (often a 3-cycle). Insert commutators into the skeleton to solve the remaining pieces, searching for maximum cancellation with existing moves.

pros: Dramatically expands the search space for efficient solutions
Often reveals blocks and pairs invisible from one direction alone
Used by virtually all top FMC solvers
Can save 5-10 moves compared to single-direction approaches
Elegant mathematical foundation
cons: Mentally demanding — requires tracking moves on two scrambles
Easy to make errors when switching directions
Requires practice to develop intuition for when to switch
Not a standalone method — must be combined with blockbuilding
Adds complexity to an already complex FMC process

freefop.description: A flexible variant of CFOP that relaxes the rigid cross-first approach. FreeFOP allows solvers to build a pseudo cross (where cross edges may not be in their exact final positions) and use pseudo-slotting techniques during F2L, correcting alignment at the end. This freedom enables more efficient solutions by taking advantage of whatever favorable configurations appear in the scramble rather than forcing a fixed sequence.

steps.description: Build a cross-like structure on the bottom face, but with relaxed placement rules. Cross edges may be offset by a U-layer adjustment, or the cross may be built on a non-standard face. The key idea is solving whichever cross is most efficient rather than a predetermined one.

Insert corner-edge pairs into their slots, but with the freedom to use pseudo-slotting: pairs may go into non-standard slots and be corrected later. Solvers can also build pairs across different slot positions and adjust at the end of F2L.
Orient all pieces on the last layer so the top face is one solid color. Standard OLL algorithms apply, identical to CFOP.
Permute all last layer pieces to their correct positions. The AUF (Adjust Upper Face) at the end may be more complex if a pseudo offset was used throughout the solve.

pros: More efficient than rigid CFOP due to flexible cross and F2L
Fewer cube rotations by choosing convenient slots
Encourages creative and adaptive solving
Lower move count than standard CFOP (~48-55 moves)
Builds deeper cube understanding
cons: Harder to plan cross and F2L during inspection
Less systematic than CFOP, making it harder to practice consistently
AUF can be tricky with pseudo offsets
Very few dedicated tutorials or resources
Requires strong cube visualization skills

guimond.description: The Guimond method is a 2x2 speedsolving approach developed by Gaetan Guimond. It works by first orienting one face, then orienting the opposite face while separating the layers, and finally permuting all pieces. It avoids the need for a full CLL algorithm set by breaking the solve into orientation and permutation phases.

steps.description: Orient one face of the cube so that all four stickers on that face share the same color. This is done intuitively and only requires orienting pieces, not placing them in the correct position.
Orient the opposite face so all four stickers match in color, while simultaneously separating the top and bottom layer pieces so each layer contains pieces from only one set.
Permute all pieces on both layers to reach the solved state. Since all pieces are already oriented and separated, only PBL-style algorithms are needed.

pros: Low algorithm count compared to CLL/EG methods
Orientation-first approach can be very fast with practice
Good stepping stone toward more advanced 2x2 methods
Consistent move count across solves
cons: Slightly higher move count than CLL-based methods
Layer separation can be tricky to learn initially
Not as widely used at the top level as EG or CLL
Recognition for the second step takes practice

hawaiian-kociemba.description: Hawaiian Kociemba is a method inspired by Herbert Kociemba's two-phase computer algorithm, adapted for human solving. The method starts by orienting all edges and forming an 'arrow' pattern on the first face, then completes the solve using a restricted RUL moveset. It is named for its origin in the Hawaiian cubing community. The approach is relatively accessible while achieving a respectable move count.

steps.description: Orient all 12 edges and simultaneously create an 'arrow' shape on the bottom face — the cross center plus one correctly placed edge and its adjacent corner-edge pair. This step establishes the foundation while ensuring all edges are oriented for an efficient finish.

Complete the first two layers using only R, U, and L moves (plus their inverses and double turns). Since all edges are oriented, F2L pairs can be built and inserted without F or B moves, similar to ZZ's F2L step.
Solve the last layer. With edges already oriented, the last layer is reduced to OCLL (orient corners) followed by PLL, or can be solved with ZBLL for a single-look finish.

pros: Approachable for intermediate solvers
Good move count (~40 moves)
Rotation-free F2L after EO
Ergonomic RUL moveset
Interesting bridge between beginner and advanced methods
cons: EO arrow step requires practice to plan efficiently
Fewer resources than mainstream methods
Not widely used competitively
Limited community development
EO recognition can be tricky for beginners

hd-method.description: The HD (Higgs-Demars) method is an advanced 2x2 speedsolving technique. It involves solving one face first, then using HD-specific algorithms to complete the puzzle. The method leverages a streamlined algorithm set related to CLL, allowing for very low move counts and fast solves.

steps.description: Solve one complete face of the 2x2 cube. Unlike the Ortega method, pieces should be correctly permuted relative to each other on this face, forming a proper layer.
Apply HD algorithms to solve the remaining layer in one step. These algorithms simultaneously orient and permute all remaining pieces, similar to CLL but with HD-specific optimizations.

pros: Very low move count (11-15 moves average)
One-look second step for fast finishes
Builds on CLL knowledge
Efficient for one-handed solving
cons: Requires learning a significant number of algorithms
First face must be solved correctly (not just oriented)
Less widely documented than Ortega or EG
Recognition can be difficult at first

heise.description: A fully intuitive method created by Ryan Heise in 2003 that requires zero algorithms. The Heise method builds four 1x2x2 blocks, orients edges, then uses commutators to finish the solve. It achieves an impressively low move count through pure understanding of cube mechanics rather than memorized sequences. Regarded as one of the most elegant methods in cubing, it is primarily used for fewest-moves challenges and by those seeking a deep understanding of the puzzle.

steps.description: Create four separate 1x2x2 blocks (each consisting of one corner and one edge) and arrange them so that two blocks share an edge on each side. This forms the skeleton of the solve while leaving flexibility for efficient solutions.
Orient all remaining edges that are not part of the four blocks. Edge orientation is defined relative to the F/B axis, and this step ensures all edges can be solved without F or B moves.

Place all remaining edges and two of the remaining corners into their correct positions. This is done intuitively by matching edges to blocks and inserting corner-edge pairs. After this step, only three corners remain unsolved.

Solve the final three corners using a commutator. A commutator is a sequence of moves in the form A B A' B' that cycles three pieces without disturbing the rest of the cube. This step requires understanding of how corner pieces move.

pros: Zero algorithms required — fully intuitive
Very low move count (~35-45 moves)
Develops deep understanding of cube mechanics
Excellent for FMC (Fewest Moves Challenge)
Elegant and satisfying to use
cons: Extremely difficult to learn and master
Very slow turning speed due to constant thinking
No competitive speedsolving results
Requires strong understanding of commutators
Almost no structured tutorials available

hoya.description: An alternative 4x4 speedsolving method developed by Jong-Ho Jeong (known as Hoya). Unlike Yau, Hoya solves opposite centers first, then all four cross edges, then the remaining centers, before proceeding to edge pairing and a standard 3x3 finish. This order makes center building easier since cross edges are already placed, and offers a flexible, intuitive approach to 4x4 solving.

steps.description: Solve two opposite centers (typically white and yellow). Starting with just two centers gives maximum freedom for piece placement and efficient commutators.
Pair and place all 4 cross dedges (double-edges) into their correct positions relative to the solved centers. Having only two centers solved provides enough freedom to pair edges efficiently.
Complete the remaining 4 centers around the already-placed cross edges. This step requires careful commutators to avoid disrupting the cross.
Pair all remaining dedges using the 3-2-3 technique, the same efficient edge pairing approach used in the Yau method. Place 3 edges in a slice, pair them, then handle 2 and 3 more.
With the cube reduced to a 3x3 state and the cross already complete, solve the first two layers by inserting corner-edge pairs into their slots using standard CFOP techniques.
Orient all pieces on the last layer so the top face is one color. OLL parity may need to be resolved on 4x4.
Permute all last layer pieces into their correct positions to complete the solve. PLL parity may also occur.

pros: Centers are easier to build after cross edges are placed
Flexible and intuitive step ordering
Good for one-handed 4x4 solving
Shares many techniques with Yau, making it easy to switch
cons: Less popular than Yau, so fewer tutorials and resources
Slightly less efficient cross building than Yau
Edge pairing step is identical but center step can be awkward
Smaller competitive community

human-thistlethwaite.description: A human-adapted version of Morwen Thistlethwaite's famous computer algorithm. The original algorithm was the first to prove the cube could always be solved in a bounded number of moves by progressively restricting the available move set. The Human Thistlethwaite Algorithm (HTA) translates this concept into four manageable phases that a human can execute, reducing the cube step by step until it can be solved with only half-turn moves.

steps.description: Orient all 12 edges so that they can be solved without using quarter-turn F or B moves. This restricts the cube from the full move group <R, L, U, D, F, B> to <R, L, U, D, F2, B2>.
Orient all 8 corners and move the 4 E-slice (equatorial) edges into the E-slice (they do not need to be permuted correctly yet). After this phase the cube is restricted to <R, L, U2, D2, F2, B2>.

Place all pieces into their correct orbits: corners into their correct tetrads, edges into their correct slices, and fix corner parity. After this phase, the cube is restricted to <R2, L2, U2, D2, F2, B2> (half turns only).
Solve the cube completely using only half-turn (180-degree) moves. Since all pieces are in their correct orbits, the cube can be solved with only R2, L2, U2, D2, F2, B2 moves.

pros: Methodical and logical approach to solving
Good move count (~40-45 moves)
Teaches important group theory concepts
Each phase strictly reduces the problem
Interesting alternative to layer-based methods
cons: Phase transitions require careful recognition
Not competitive for speedsolving
Steep learning curve for understanding the theory
Few dedicated tutorials compared to mainstream methods
Phase 2 and 3 recognition can be challenging

jq-method.description: The JQ Method is an advanced Square-1 solving technique created by Jack Triton. It combines cubeshape solving with block building and an algorithmic finish, offering an alternative approach to the standard Vandenbergh or Lin methods. The method leverages efficient cubeshape algorithms, intuitive block building, and a streamlined algorithmic endgame.

steps.description: Transform the Square-1 from its scrambled shape back into a cube. This step uses cubeshape algorithms to bring the puzzle into a solvable cubic form.
Build blocks of correctly paired and positioned pieces using intuitive techniques. This phase organizes pieces into useful groupings that simplify the algorithmic finish.
Complete the solve using algorithms to handle edge permutation and parity. This step resolves all remaining pieces using known algorithm sets.

pros: Block building phase allows creative and efficient solving
Lower slice count than some traditional methods
Good for solvers who prefer intuitive middle phases
Algorithmic finish provides consistency
cons: Requires strong understanding of Square-1 mechanics
Block building step has high variability between solves
Fewer community resources compared to Vandenbergh
Parity handling adds complexity

keyhole-pyra.description: A beginner-to-intermediate Pyraminx method that uses a keyhole technique. The solver leaves one position open (the 'keyhole') while inserting edges one at a time, then solves the final edges. This method is very intuitive and requires minimal algorithms, making it a popular choice for those transitioning from absolute beginner methods. The keyhole concept is easy to grasp and applies fundamental puzzle-solving logic.

steps.description: Twist all four tips to match their adjacent center colors. This trivial step can be done at any time during the solve.

Choose a bottom edge position as the keyhole (open slot). Solve two of the three bottom edges by using the open keyhole position to cycle edges without disturbing already-solved pieces. Insert edges one at a time, always keeping one slot open as a working space.

Close the keyhole and solve the remaining edges. This involves placing the last bottom edge and simultaneously ensuring the top edges are correct. A few simple algorithms or intuitive moves handle the final cases.

pros: Very intuitive — minimal algorithms needed
Keyhole concept is easy to understand
Good stepping stone to L4E and other advanced methods
Flexible and forgiving approach
cons: Higher move count than L4E or Oka
Not used competitively at high levels
Keyhole management adds extra moves
Limited speed ceiling

l4e-method.description: The most popular intermediate-to-advanced Pyraminx speedcubing method. L4E works by first solving one edge intuitively to create a V-shape (a solved edge plus its two adjacent centers), then solving the remaining four edges simultaneously using a set of algorithms. With optimized algorithms and good recognition, L4E is very fast and is used by many top Pyraminx solvers.

steps.description: Twist the four trivial tips to match their adjacent center colors. This can be done at any point during the solve but is typically handled first or concurrently with other steps.

Solve one edge to create a V-shape — two connected faces that are each partially complete. This is done intuitively and quickly. The V consists of one solved edge flanked by its two matching centers (which are always solved on Pyraminx).

Solve the remaining four edges in one step using L4E algorithms. There are approximately 16 cases (excluding mirrors and inverses). Recognition involves checking the four unsolved edges' colors and positions. This is the core of the method and where speed is gained.

pros: Very fast method — used by top Pyraminx competitors
Relatively few algorithms (~16 L4E cases)
Simple structure: V + one-look finish
Low move count
Well-documented with many resources
cons: L4E recognition can be tricky at first
V selection requires color neutrality for optimal efficiency
Some L4E algorithms are awkward to execute
Marginally slower than Oka or Bell for the very fastest solvers

lbl-2x2.description: The beginner method for the 2x2 cube. It applies the same layer-by-layer logic used in beginner 3x3 methods but simplified for the 2x2. The solver first completes one layer (both face color and correct permutation), then orients the last layer corners, and finally permutes them. It requires very few algorithms and is the easiest way to learn to solve a 2x2.

steps.description: Solve one complete layer of the 2x2 cube. All four bottom pieces must be correctly oriented and positioned relative to each other. Start with one corner, then insert the remaining three corners one at a time using simple intuitive moves (R U R' or similar triggers).

Orient the four top-layer corners so the top face is a solid color. Use the Sune algorithm (R U R' U R U2 R') repeatedly to twist corners into the correct orientation. This may need to be applied multiple times from different angles.

Move the top-layer corners into their correct positions while keeping their orientation. Use a corner-cycling algorithm to swap pieces into place. The typical algorithm is a variation of the A-perm or a simple corner 3-cycle.

pros: Very easy to learn with only 2-3 algorithms
Uses the same logic as beginner 3x3 methods
Great for absolute beginners
No complex recognition required
Good foundation for understanding the 2x2
cons: Very slow compared to Ortega, CLL, or EG
High move count for a 2x2 method
Three-step last layer is inefficient
No competitive potential

lbl-mega.description: The standard method for solving the Megaminx, applying layer-by-layer logic to the dodecahedral puzzle. Like the 3x3 LBL method but adapted for 12 faces instead of 6, the solver works from one face down to the opposite face, solving one layer of faces at a time. The method involves solving the first face, then the surrounding star and edges, working through the middle layers, and finally handling the last face. Most Megaminx solvers use some variation of LBL, making it the foundation of Megaminx solving.

steps.description: Solve the first face of the Megaminx completely — the center is fixed, so solve the five edges (forming a star) and five corners. This is done intuitively by inserting edges and then corners, similar to solving the first layer of a 3x3.

Solve the five faces adjacent to the first face by inserting edge-corner pairs, similar to F2L on a 3x3. Work around the Megaminx, solving the band of five faces one at a time. Each face requires placing its edges and corners relative to the already-solved portions.

Place the remaining edges in the equatorial region and middle layers. Use edge-insertion algorithms to place edges without disturbing the solved top half. This is similar to middle-layer edge insertion on a 3x3.

Solve the five edges of the last face to form a star. This involves orienting and then permuting the last five edges. Algorithms similar to 3x3 last layer edge techniques are used but adapted for five edges.

Solve the five corners of the last face by orienting and then permuting them. This completes the Megaminx. Corner orientation uses Sune-type algorithms, and permutation uses corner-cycling algorithms.

pros: Most intuitive approach to the Megaminx
Uses familiar 3x3 techniques adapted for the Megaminx
Clear step-by-step progression
Well-documented with many tutorials
Foundation for all Megaminx solving
cons: High total move count due to the puzzle's complexity
Many pieces make the solve long (2-5 minutes typical)
Last layer can be slow with basic algorithms
Requires patience and good piece tracking

lbl.description: The classic layer-by-layer method that most cubers learn as their very first approach to solving the Rubik's Cube. LBL breaks the solve into 7 clearly defined steps, solving the cube one layer at a time from bottom to top. Each step uses only 1-2 short algorithms, making it extremely accessible. While it shares DNA with the beginner's method, this formulation emphasizes the full 7-step breakdown with explicit cross, corners, and edges stages for each layer.

steps.description: Form a plus sign (cross) on the first layer (usually white), ensuring each edge piece matches the center color of its adjacent face. This establishes the foundation of the solve.
Insert the 4 corner pieces of the first layer into their correct positions. Each corner is placed by positioning it above its target slot and using a short trigger algorithm.
Insert the 4 edge pieces of the middle layer into their correct positions. Two mirror algorithms handle inserting edges to the left or right.
Form a cross on the last layer (yellow face) by orienting the 4 edge pieces so yellow faces up. This step does not worry about edge positions, only orientation.
Move the yellow cross edges to their correct positions so each edge matches the center color of its adjacent face. The yellow cross is now correctly aligned.
Move the last layer corners to their correct positions (the correct corner in each slot), without worrying about their orientation. Find one correctly positioned corner and cycle the others.
Orient the last layer corners so that yellow faces up on all of them, completing the cube. Each corner is twisted in place using a repeated trigger without disturbing the rest of the solve.

pros: Very easy to understand with clear visual progress at each step
Requires only 6-8 short algorithms total
Step-by-step approach minimizes confusion
Perfect first method for absolute beginners
Abundant tutorials in every format and language
cons: Very high move count (~120-150 moves)
Slow solve times even when well-practiced
No natural progression path toward speed
Many separate steps make the solve feel lengthy
Layer restriction prevents efficient piece placement

leor.description: LEOR is a hybrid method that combines elements of Roux-style blockbuilding with ZZ-style edge orientation. It builds a 1x2x3 first block on the left side, then orients all edges while placing E-slice and D-layer edges (EOStripe), builds the right block, and finishes with a last layer that benefits from pre-oriented edges. LEOR offers an interesting middle ground between Roux and ZZ, inheriting strengths from both methods.

steps.description: Build a 1x2x3 block on the left side of the cube, identical to the Roux first block. This block contains the DL edge and two corner-edge pairs.

Orient all remaining edges while placing key edges into the E-slice and bottom layer to form a 'stripe' pattern. This step transitions the solve from blockbuilding into an EO-based approach, ensuring all subsequent moves can avoid F and B quarter turns.
Build the right-side 1x2x3 block to complete the first two layers. Since all edges are oriented, this step uses only R and U moves, making it very ergonomic and rotation-free.
Solve the last layer with all edges already oriented. This can be done with OCLL + PLL, COLL + EPLL, or full ZBLL for a one-look finish.

pros: Combines strengths of Roux and ZZ
Low move count (~42-50 moves)
Rotation-free F2L after EOStripe
Ergonomic RU moveset for right block
Flexible last layer options including ZBLL
cons: EOStripe step is unique and requires dedicated practice
Fewer resources than Roux or ZZ individually
First block to EO transition can be awkward
Small community compared to mainstream methods
Planning EOStripe in inspection is difficult

lin-method.description: An alternative Square-1 method inspired by blockbuilding concepts. The Lin method takes a fundamentally different approach from the standard Vandenbergh method by focusing on pairing blocks during cubeshape and solving in fewer, more integrated steps. It pairs edges and corners during the cubeshape phase, then uses a CSP (Cubeshape Parity) aware approach to solve the last layer. The method offers lower move counts but requires more advanced planning and recognition.

steps.description: Restore the cube shape while simultaneously pairing edge-corner blocks. Unlike Vandenbergh where cubeshape is purely about restoring the shape, the Lin method aims to create useful blocks during this phase. This requires deeper understanding of how pieces move during slash moves.

Handle the cubeshape parity situation. Parity on Square-1 is determined during cubeshape, and the Lin method addresses it explicitly at this stage rather than deferring it to the end. This step may involve specific algorithms or adjustments depending on the parity state.

Solve the remaining pieces of the last layer. Because blocks were paired during cubeshape, the last layer has fewer pieces to solve. This step uses algorithms to orient and permute the remaining corners and edges in as few looks as possible.

pros: Lower overall move count than Vandenbergh
Fewer total steps when blocks are paired efficiently
Parity is handled earlier, reducing end-of-solve complexity
Rewards strong planning and inspection skills
cons: Much harder inspection planning than Vandenbergh
Fewer resources and tutorials available
Blockbuilding during cubeshape is difficult to master
Less structured than Vandenbergh, requiring more flexibility

linear.description: A beginner-to-intermediate Clock method that solves each clock face one at a time in a fixed, linear order. The solver works through each of the 14 clock faces (9 front, 9 back, sharing 4 corner clocks) systematically, pressing pins to isolate individual faces and turning wheels to set each one to 12 o'clock. While not as fast as the simultaneous method, the linear approach is straightforward and easy to learn, making it the standard beginner method for Clock.

steps.description: Solve the four corner clock faces on the front side one at a time. For each corner clock, press the pins to isolate that clock face, then turn the nearest wheel to set it to 12 o'clock. Work through all four corners systematically.
Solve the four edge clock faces on the front side. Each edge clock is between two corner clocks. Press pins to isolate two adjacent corners and turn the wheel between them to set the edge clock.
Solve the center clock face on the front. Press all pins to the down position and turn any wheel to adjust the center. The center clock is affected by all four wheels when all pins are down.

Flip the Clock and repeat the process for the back side: solve corner clocks, edge clocks, and the center clock in order. Be mindful that back-side turns can affect front-side clocks through shared pins.

pros: Easy to learn and understand
Systematic and predictable approach
Good introduction to Clock mechanics
Minimal memorization required
Each step is simple and isolated
cons: Slow compared to the simultaneous method
Many individual moves add up to a high total
Back-side solving can disrupt front-side clocks
Not competitive for speedcubing at high levels

lmcf.description: LMCF (Low Movecount Corners First) is a corners-first method optimized for minimal move count. It solves all eight corners first, then places the E-layer edges (E2L), and finishes with the last five or six edges (L5E/L6E). Unlike the classic Waterman corners-first approach, LMCF uses modern techniques to achieve an impressively low move count while remaining relatively accessible. It is popular among cubers interested in efficient solving without heavy algorithm memorization.

steps.description: Solve all eight corners of the cube. This typically involves solving the first layer corners intuitively, then using CLL or Ortega-style techniques to orient and permute the last layer corners.
Place the four equatorial (E-slice / middle layer) edges into their correct positions. With all corners solved, these edges can be inserted using M and U moves without disturbing the corners.
Solve the remaining five or six edges (depending on whether one edge was solved during E2L). This step uses M and U moves to orient and permute the remaining edges, completing the solve.

pros: Low move count (~35-45 moves)
Fewer algorithms than CFOP
Corners-first gives unique cube understanding
Ergonomic M and U moves in later steps
Good introduction to corners-first solving
cons: Corners-first thinking is unfamiliar to most solvers
L5E/L6E can be slow without practice
Not competitive for top-level speedsolving
Limited resources compared to mainstream methods
Edge steps require good M-slice fluency

m2-r2.description: An intermediate 3BLD (3x3 blindfolded) method that improves upon Old Pochmann by using M2 moves for edges and R2 moves for corners. Instead of setup + T-perm for every edge, M2 uses a simpler M2 move to swap the buffer edge with the target (after setup moves), cutting the execution move count roughly in half compared to Old Pochmann. R2 does the same for corners using R2-based swaps. The most popular combination is M2 for edges with Old Pochmann (Y-perm) for corners, often called M2/OP.

steps.description: Memorize the edge cycles using the same letter-tracing method as Old Pochmann. The buffer is typically the DF edge (for M2). Trace cycles through all edge sticker positions, generating a sequence of letters to memorize.
Memorize corner cycles using letter tracing. The corner buffer is typically UBL (for Old Pochmann corners) or UFR (for R2 corners). Trace cycles through all corner sticker positions.

Solve corners using either R2-based swaps or Old Pochmann Y-perm swaps. For R2, the buffer is at UFR, and each target is set up, then an R2 move is performed to swap the buffer with the target. Many solvers prefer using Old Pochmann (Y-perm) for corners as it is more straightforward, making this M2/OP rather than M2/R2.

Solve edges using M2-based swaps. The buffer is DF. For each target letter, perform a setup move to bring the target to the UB position, then execute M2 to swap it with the buffer, then undo the setup. The M2 swap is much shorter than a T-perm, resulting in significantly faster execution. Special handling is needed for targets in the M-slice (UF and UB positions).

pros: Significantly faster execution than Old Pochmann
M2 edge swaps are quick and ergonomic
Good stepping stone between Old Pochmann and 3-style
Lower move count than OP (roughly 60-140 total)
Widely used at intermediate competitive BLD level
cons: M-slice special cases can cause errors
R2 corners are tricky — many solvers stick with OP corners
Still much slower than 3-style for advanced solvers
Setup moves still add significant move count
Must learn new setup moves compared to Old Pochmann

mehta.description: A modern speedcubing method created by Yash Mehta in 2020 that blends block-building with algorithmic steps to achieve a very low move count. Mehta uses a unique sequence: build a first block, solve three quarter-belt edges, orient edges while placing the last edge, solve the DR corner and orient remaining corners, then finish with ZBLL. It is one of the newest competitive methods and represents a fresh approach to 3x3 solving.

steps.description: Build a 1x2x3 block on the left side of the cube, similar to the first block in Roux. This block contains the DL edge and two corner-edge pairs.
Solve 3 of the 4 edges in the E-layer belt (the equatorial slice), leaving one edge unsolved. This step positions the cube for efficient edge orientation.
Orient all remaining edges on the cube while simultaneously placing the last E-layer edge. After this step, all edges are oriented, unlocking efficient corner-solving algorithms.
Solve the DR (down-right) edge position and orient all remaining corners. This leaves the cube in a state solvable by ZBLL or a similar last-layer algorithm set.
Solve the last layer in one look using ZBLL algorithms. Since all edges are oriented, only ZBLL cases apply. This step completes the entire solve.

pros: Very low move count (~40-45 moves)
Ergonomic fingertricks throughout
Fewer cube rotations than CFOP
Novel approach that avoids traditional F2L structure
Strong potential as method theory continues to develop
cons: Very new method with limited competitive results
Fewer resources and tutorials compared to CFOP or Roux
Complex recognition for EOLE and TDR steps
Steep learning curve for the full algorithm sets
Requires ZBLL knowledge for optimal performance

meyer.description: The Meyer method is an advanced 4x4 speedsolving technique that adapts the Roux philosophy to big cubes. Instead of reducing to a CFOP-style 3x3 solve, Meyer builds a Roux-style block structure after centers and edge pairing. It begins by solving R and L centers, building a 1x3x4 block, solving the remaining centers, pairing edges, and finishing with a Roux-based 3x3 stage including CMLL.

steps.description: Solve the right and left centers of the 4x4 cube. These centers form the foundation for the Roux-style block building that follows.
Build a 1x3x4 block on one side, combining a center column with paired edges and corner-edge pairs. This is analogous to the first Roux block but adapted for 4x4.
Solve the remaining four centers (F, B, U, D) without disturbing the completed block and R/L centers.
Pair all remaining edges while preserving the solved centers and block. This reduces the puzzle to a Roux-solvable 3x3 state.
Complete the solve using Roux method techniques on the now-reduced 3x3 state. This includes finishing the second block, CMLL, and last six edges.

pros: Lower move count than Yau for Roux users
Leverages existing Roux skills for 4x4 solving
Creative and flexible block-building phase
Good rotationless solving potential
cons: Steeper learning curve than Yau or reduction
Fewer resources and tutorials available
Block building on 4x4 requires strong spatial reasoning
Edge pairing while preserving blocks can be tricky

monkeydude.description: The Monkeydude1313 method is a beginner-friendly Skewb solving approach. It works in three phases: solving the first layer, then the centers, and finally applying an algorithm for the last four corners (L4C). Its straightforward structure makes it an excellent entry point for Skewb speedsolving.

steps.description: Solve one complete face and its adjacent centers, forming the first layer of the Skewb. This is done intuitively by placing corners one at a time on the bottom face.
Orient and position the remaining center pieces to match their adjacent solved corners. This step uses simple, short algorithms to cycle centers into place.
Solve the last four corners on the top layer using a single algorithm or short sequence. Since the first layer and centers are complete, only the top corners remain.

pros: Very easy to learn for beginners
Low algorithm count
Clear step-by-step structure with no ambiguity
Good foundation for learning more advanced Skewb methods
cons: Higher move count than intermediate methods like Sarah
Not competitive at the top level
Limited optimization potential
First layer solving can be slow without practice

nautilus.description: A hybrid blockbuilding and algorithmic method for the 3x3. Nautilus builds a 1x2x3 block (similar to Roux's first block), then solves the remaining pieces using a combination of last slot and last layer techniques. It blends the intuitive blockbuilding of Roux with algorithmic efficiency, offering a unique solving experience that emphasizes both creativity and speed.

steps.description: Build a 1x2x3 block on the left side of the cube, typically on the D-layer. This block includes the DL edge, and two corner-edge pairs forming a 1x2x3 shape. This step is identical to Roux first block building.

Build a 1x2x2 square (a 'square' consisting of one corner-edge pair plus the DR edge) on the right side of the bottom layer. This partially completes the second block without fully extending it to a full 1x2x3.

Solve the last F2L slot (the remaining corner-edge pair on the right side) and the last layer in fewer steps than traditional approaches. Various sub-methods can be used here including ZBLS + ZBLL, Winter Variation + PLL, or other LSLL approaches. The key idea is to influence the last layer state while inserting the last pair.

pros: Lower move count than CFOP due to efficient blockbuilding
Flexible and creative first two steps
LSLL approach can skip OLL entirely with enough algorithm knowledge
Fewer rotations than CFOP
cons: Requires strong blockbuilding intuition
LSLL sub-methods require many algorithms (especially ZBLL)
Fewer resources and community support compared to mainstream methods
Inspection planning is more demanding

nutella.description: The Nutella method is an advanced Pyraminx speedsolving technique known for its low move count and creative solving approach. It begins by solving the top layer with a specific 3-color front face constraint, then simultaneously solves centers and the top, and finishes with L3E. The method's strength lies in its efficiency and the simultaneous nature of its middle step.

steps.description: Solve the top layer while ensuring the front face displays exactly three colors. This constraint sets up the puzzle for an efficient simultaneous solve in the next step.
Solve the centers and complete the top layer simultaneously. This step exploits the 3-color front constraint to solve multiple elements at once, keeping the move count very low.
Solve the final three edges using L3E algorithms. The setup from previous steps often results in favorable L3E cases.

pros: Very low move count (9-13 moves average)
Simultaneous solving step is highly efficient
Competitive at the highest level of Pyraminx solving
Elegant and creative approach to the puzzle
cons: Requires strong understanding of Pyraminx mechanics
3-color front constraint takes time to master
Fewer learning resources compared to LBL or top-first methods
Recognition for the simultaneous step is difficult to develop

oka.description: An advanced Pyraminx method developed by Yusuf Oka. The Oka method focuses on solving a top layer (or block) first, then using optimized algorithms to finish the remaining edges in one look. It is designed for high-level Pyraminx speedcubing and emphasizes efficient blockbuilding and low move counts. The method allows for excellent lookahead and planning during inspection.

steps.description: Twist all four tips to match their adjacent centers. Trivial step done at any point.
