Solve the top layer by building a complete top with all three edges correctly placed and oriented around the top tip. This creates a fully solved top face and the three edges connecting to it.

Solve the three remaining bottom-layer edges using algorithms. Since the top is fully solved, the bottom edges need to be cycled and/or flipped into position. This step has a small number of cases that can be solved in one look.

pros: Very low move count overall
Full top can be planned in inspection
Clean two-phase structure (top then bottom)
Highly competitive at the top level
cons: Requires excellent inspection planning
Color neutrality is almost essential
Limited resources compared to L4E
Top layer must be fully solved, which can be harder than building a V

old-pochmann.description: The standard beginner method for 3x3 blindfolded solving (3BLD), created by Stefan Pochmann. Old Pochmann (OP) uses a simple two-piece swapping technique to solve edges and corners one at a time. The solver memorizes the positions of all pieces, then executes a series of setup moves followed by a single swap algorithm (T-perm for edges, Y-perm for corners) to place each piece. Its simplicity and reliability make it the most recommended first 3BLD method.

steps.description: Memorize the cycle of all 12 edges. Starting from a buffer position (typically the UF or DF edge), trace where each piece needs to go, forming a sequence of letters or images. Each edge sticker is assigned a letter, and the solver traces the cycle: buffer goes to target, target's occupant becomes the next target, and so on until the cycle closes. Multiple cycles may be needed.

Memorize the cycle of all 8 corners using the same letter-tracing technique. Starting from a buffer position (typically the UFR or UBL corner), trace the corner cycles. Corners have three stickers each, so the letter assigned depends on which sticker faces which direction.

Solve all corners by executing the memorized sequence. For each target in the memo, perform a setup move to bring the target piece to a specific position, execute the Y-perm (or another corner-swapping algorithm), then undo the setup move. This swaps the buffer piece with the target piece. Repeat for each letter in the corner memo.

Solve all edges using the same setup-swap-undo technique. For each target in the edge memo, perform setup moves to bring the target edge to a specific position, execute the T-perm (or another edge-swapping algorithm), then reverse the setup moves. Repeat for the entire edge memo sequence.

pros: Simplest 3BLD execution method — only 2 algorithms needed (T-perm and Y-perm)
Very reliable and easy to understand
Widely documented with many tutorials
Good foundation for understanding BLD solving concepts
Low error rate once mastered
cons: Very high move count (100-200+ total moves)
Slow execution compared to M2/R2 or 3-style
Setup moves add significant time
Not competitive for serious 3BLD speedcubing
Execution phase can be tediously long

one-flip.description: The 1-Flip method is an intermediate Pyraminx speedsolving technique. It involves solving two of the three top edges while intentionally leaving one edge flipped, then solving the centers, and finally finishing all remaining edges. The deliberate misorientation in the first step creates favorable cases for the final step, balancing ease of planning with overall efficiency.

steps.description: Solve two of the three top-layer edges correctly while deliberately leaving the third edge flipped (incorrectly oriented). This intentional flip simplifies later steps and is planned during inspection.
Twist the tips to solve all center pieces. This aligns the centers with the partially-solved top layer.
Solve the remaining edges including the intentionally flipped edge from step one. The flipped edge creates specific cases that can be solved efficiently with L3E-related techniques.

pros: Easy first step that can be fully planned in inspection
Intentional flip creates favorable finishing cases
Accessible learning curve for intermediate solvers
Good stepping stone toward advanced Pyraminx methods
cons: Slightly higher move count than Nutella method
The concept of intentional misorientation can be unintuitive at first
Not as widely used as WO or top-first at competitions
Edge finishing step has more cases than standard L3E

orozco.description: A hybrid 3BLD method that combines M2 edge solving with 3-style corner commutators. Named after the method's popularizer, Orozco provides a middle ground between the simplicity of M2/OP and the speed of full 3-style. Edges are solved using M2-based techniques (with some 3-style edge commutators mixed in for common cases), while corners are solved using full 3-style commutators. This allows solvers to benefit from fast corner execution while maintaining the simpler M2 framework for edges during the transition to full 3-style.

steps.description: Memorize edge cycles using letter tracing with the DF buffer (standard M2 buffer). Generate letter pairs for the memo sequence. The memo technique is the same as M2 — each letter represents a target for an M2 swap, though some common targets may be handled with 3-style commutators instead.

Memorize corner cycles using letter tracing with the UBL or UFR buffer. Generate letter pairs, where each pair represents one 3-style corner commutator to execute. Visual memo with images is standard for corners.

Execute corner 3-cycles using commutator algorithms, identical to the full 3-style method. Each letter pair in the corner memo corresponds to one commutator that cycles three corners. This gives fast corner execution comparable to world-class solvers.

Execute edge swaps using primarily M2-based techniques, with 3-style commutators substituted for common or easy targets. The M2 framework provides a reliable backbone, while gradually incorporating commutators for specific targets improves speed. This hybrid approach allows incremental learning toward full 3-style edges.

pros: Faster than pure M2/OP due to 3-style corners
Smoother transition path toward full 3-style
M2 edges are reliable and well-understood
Allows incremental improvement as more commutators are learned
Good competitive performance at intermediate BLD level
cons: Still slower than full 3-style for edges
Hybrid approach means two different execution styles to manage
M2 special cases still apply for edge targets
Eventually should be upgraded to full 3-style for top performance

ortega.description: The standard intermediate method for 2x2 speedcubing, also known as the Varasano method. The solver first completes one face (without worrying about correct permutation), then orients the opposite face, and finally permutes both layers simultaneously using PBL algorithms. With only about 12 algorithms total, Ortega is the go-to method for cubers looking to break sub-5 on 2x2 before learning CLL or EG.

steps.description: Solve one face of the 2x2 cube so all four stickers are the same color. Importantly, the four pieces do NOT need to be in their correct positions relative to each other — only the color orientation of one face matters. This makes the step very fast and flexible.

Orient the opposite face so all four stickers match, creating a solid color on top. There are 7 OLL cases for the 2x2 (excluding the solved case). These are short algorithms that are easy to learn and fast to execute.

Permute both layers simultaneously to complete the solve. Both faces now have solid colors, and you need to rearrange the pieces so all sides are uniform. There are 6 PBL cases (including the solved and AUF-only cases). This is the defining step of Ortega.

pros: Very few algorithms to learn (approximately 12 total)
Fast to learn and implement
Good stepping stone toward CLL and EG methods
First face is very flexible since permutation does not matter
Achievable sub-4 times with practice
cons: Not as fast as CLL or EG for top-level solving
PBL step can feel slow compared to one-look methods
Speed ceiling around sub-3 for most solvers
Two-look last layer after face solve is inherently slower than one-look approaches

petrus.description: One of the earliest speedcubing methods, based on blockbuilding. Solves a 2x2x2 block, expands it, then finishes with fewer algorithms than CFOP.

steps.description: Build a 2x2x2 block in any corner of the cube.
Expand the 2x2x2 block into a 2x2x3 block.
Orient the remaining bad edges while preserving the 2x2x3 block.
Complete the first two layers using the oriented edges advantage.
Solve the last layer with oriented edges (similar to ZZ last layer).

pros: Low move count
Deep understanding of cube mechanics
Edge orientation advantage
Good for FMC
cons: Not as fast as CFOP for speedsolving
Block building is slow at first
Limited modern resources
Higher skill floor

pochmann-clock.description: The Pochmann method (also known as Clochmann) is a beginner-friendly Clock solving technique. It solves the puzzle in four phases: front cross, flip, back cross, and corners. This structured approach makes it easy to learn and provides a clear framework for solving the Clock puzzle from start to finish.

steps.description: Solve the four edge clocks on the front face to form a cross pattern. Each edge clock is set to the correct time by pressing the appropriate pins and turning the wheels.
Flip the Clock puzzle over to access the back face. This is a physical step that transitions from front-face solving to back-face solving.
Solve the four edge clocks on the back face, forming a cross on this side. The technique mirrors the front cross step but on the opposite face.
Solve all four corner clocks. Since the crosses on both faces are already solved, the corners can be adjusted using specific pin and wheel combinations.

pros: Easy to learn for Clock beginners
Clear four-phase structure with no ambiguity
Good foundation for learning more advanced Clock methods
Consistent and reliable solving approach
cons: Flip adds time compared to no-flip methods
Not competitive at the highest level
Sequential approach limits optimization potential
Back cross can accidentally disturb front face work

r2.description: The R2 method is an intermediate blindfolded solving technique specifically for 3BLD corner pieces. It uses R2 as the primary swapping move to cycle corners into their correct positions, analogous to how the M2 method handles edges. Together, M2 (edges) and R2 (corners) form a complete and accessible 3BLD solving system.

steps.description: Memorize the corner cycle sequence using a letter scheme. Each corner sticker is assigned a letter, and the solver traces the cycle of corners that need to be swapped, creating a sequence of letter pairs to remember.

Execute the memorized corner sequence using R2 as the swapping move. Each target is set up to the swap position, R2 is performed, and then the setup moves are reversed. Special cases exist for certain buffer-adjacent targets.
Pair R2 corner execution with M2 edge execution for a complete 3BLD solve. Typically edges are solved first with M2, followed by corners with R2.

pros: Accessible entry point for BLD corner solving
Pairs naturally with M2 for a complete BLD system
Consistent and systematic approach to corners
Lower learning barrier than commutator-based methods
cons: Slower execution than 3-style commutators
Some targets require complex setup moves
Not used at the top competitive BLD level
Special cases add complexity to an otherwise simple system

ranzha.description: The Ranzha method is an intermediate Skewb speedsolving technique created by Brandon Harnish. It emphasizes intuition-based solving rather than heavy algorithm reliance, making it a creative and flexible approach. The method builds on concepts from Sarah's method family and offers a balance between algorithmic and intuitive solving.

steps.description: Solve a complete face of the Skewb intuitively. This establishes the base layer and provides reference points for the remaining solve.
Solve or orient the corners of the opposite face using intuitive techniques. This step brings the top-layer corners into their correct positions relative to the solved first face.
Complete the solve by aligning all remaining centers. This may involve short algorithmic sequences from the Sarah family or intuitive adjustments.

pros: Encourages intuitive understanding of the Skewb
Flexible solving approach with room for creativity
Builds naturally on Sarah method knowledge
Good balance of speed and low algorithm count
cons: Intuition-based steps have variable efficiency
Less structured than purely algorithmic methods
Documentation and tutorials are limited
May not be consistent enough for top-level competition

reduction.description: The foundational method for solving big cubes (4x4 and larger). Reduction works by first solving all centers, then pairing all edges, effectively reducing the puzzle to a 3x3 state that can be finished with any standard 3x3 method. This approach scales naturally to any cube size: 4x4, 5x5, 6x6, 7x7, and beyond. While specialized methods like Yau and Hoya reorder the steps for speed, pure reduction follows a straightforward centers-then-edges sequence that is easy to understand.

steps.description: Solve all 6 center blocks of the cube. On a 4x4, each center is a 2x2 block of pieces; on a 5x5, each is a 3x3 block, and so on. Centers are solved using commutators and wide moves, typically starting with opposite faces.

Pair all edge pieces (dedges on 4x4, tredges on 5x5, etc.) so that each edge group functions as a single 3x3-style edge. On a 4x4, there are 12 edges to pair; on a 5x5, each edge has 3 pieces to group together.

With all centers solved and edges paired, the cube now behaves like a standard 3x3. Solve it using any 3x3 method (CFOP, Roux, ZZ, etc.). On even-layered cubes (4x4, 6x6), OLL parity and PLL parity may occur and require special algorithms.

pros: Scales to any cube size (4x4, 5x5, 6x6, 7x7, and beyond)
Simple concept that builds on existing 3x3 knowledge
Easy to understand the overall solving strategy
Good foundation before learning specialized big cube methods
Same framework applies to all even and odd big cubes
cons: OLL parity and PLL parity on even-layered cubes are disruptive
Many edges to pair on larger cubes makes solves lengthy
Slower than specialized methods like Yau for competition
Center building becomes tedious on 6x6 and larger
Does not optimize cross building like Yau or Hoya

roux.description: A block-building method that relies heavily on M-slice moves. Known for low move count and ergonomic turning. Growing in popularity among top solvers.

steps.description: Build a 1x2x3 block on the left side of the cube, containing the DL edge, two F2L pairs.
Build a 1x2x3 block on the right side of the cube, mirroring the first block.
Solve the corners of the last layer while ignoring M-slice edges. 42 algorithms.
Solve the remaining 6 edges using only M and U moves. Three sub-steps: EO, UL/UR, EP.

pros: Low move count (~48 moves)
Ergonomic M-slice moves
Less algorithm-heavy than CFOP
Rotationless solving
cons: Fewer resources than CFOP
Block building requires good visualization
M-slice speed needed
Harder inspection planning

sarah-advanced-method.description: The most advanced and fastest Skewb method, building on Sarah Strong's method series. The advanced method uses a one-look approach: after solving the first face, the remaining five faces are solved in a single algorithm (one-look last layer). This requires learning a large set of algorithms but results in extremely fast solves. It is the standard method used by world-class Skewb solvers and is necessary for achieving sub-3 times consistently.

steps.description: Solve one face of the Skewb with all four corners correctly colored. As with the other Sarah methods, place the face on the bottom. The four corners must show the correct color on the bottom face, but the rest of the cube will be unsolved. Plan this entirely in inspection.

Solve the entire remaining Skewb (top face corners, top center, and four side centers) in a single algorithm. This requires recognizing the exact state of all remaining pieces and applying the correct algorithm from a set of approximately 100+ cases. Recognition involves reading the top face and side stickers simultaneously.

pros: Fastest Skewb method available
One-look finish after first face is extremely efficient
Used by all top Skewb competitors
Sub-2 averages achievable by elite solvers
Clean two-step structure (face + one algorithm)
cons: Approximately 100+ algorithms to learn
Recognition is complex and takes significant practice
Large time investment to learn the full algorithm set
First face must be planned very efficiently to be competitive

sarah-beginner.description: The standard beginner method for Skewb, created by Sarah Strong. This method breaks the Skewb solve into simple, manageable steps using very few algorithms. The solver first solves one face, then positions the remaining centers, and finally solves the last layer. It is the most recommended starting method for Skewb beginners and is widely taught in tutorials.

steps.description: Solve one face of the Skewb completely. Place the center on the bottom and then solve the four corners of that face so they all match the center color. This is done intuitively using basic Skewb moves (sledgehammer: R' L R L').

Position the four remaining centers (top and four sides) into their correct locations. Use the sledgehammer move and its inverse to cycle centers without disturbing the solved bottom face. After this step, all five remaining centers are solved.

Position and orient the four remaining top-layer corners to complete the Skewb. This uses a simple corner-twisting algorithm applied from different angles. The main algorithm is a 4-move sequence (sledgehammer) applied repeatedly.

pros: Very easy to learn — only 1-2 algorithms needed
Widely taught with many tutorials available
Good introduction to Skewb mechanics
Intuitive steps build understanding of the puzzle
cons: Slow compared to intermediate and advanced methods
High move count
Last layer corners step can be repetitive
Not competitive for speedcubing

sarah-intermediate.description: The intermediate-level Skewb method by Sarah Strong, building upon the beginner method. This method solves a face on the first layer, then uses a more efficient approach for the remaining pieces by solving the top face and then finishing with a permutation step. It introduces a few more algorithms but significantly reduces solve times compared to the beginner method. It is the standard progression path for Skewb solvers before learning advanced one-look techniques.

steps.description: Solve one face of the Skewb and ensure the adjacent centers are also correctly placed. This is similar to the beginner method but with more emphasis on also getting the centers correct in fewer moves. The bottom face corners and adjacent center pieces should all be solved.
Orient the four top corners so the top face is one solid color. This is done using a set of algorithms similar to OLL on a 3x3. The Skewb has fewer cases than a cube, making this step manageable.

Permute the top corners and any remaining misplaced centers to solve the Skewb completely. This step uses permutation algorithms to move the final pieces into place without disturbing the oriented faces.

pros: Significant speed improvement over beginner method
Moderate algorithm count (approximately 10-12 total)
Clear progression from beginner Skewb method
Sub-5 times achievable with practice
cons: Not as fast as advanced one-look methods
More algorithms than the beginner method
Two-step last layer is inherently slower than one-look
Less popular than the beginner or advanced methods

screw.description: An alternative Square-1 method that takes a different approach to solving by using screw moves (repeated slash-turn sequences) as the primary mechanism. The Screw method simplifies the solving process by relying on a smaller set of intuitive move sequences. After cubeshape, the method focuses on solving corners first, then edges, using screw-like move patterns that are easy to execute and remember. It is considered a beginner-to-intermediate alternative to the Vandenbergh method.

steps.description: Restore the Square-1 to its cubic shape from any scrambled configuration. This step is the same as in other Square-1 methods — perform slash moves and layer turns to get back to a cube shape.

Solve all eight corners using a combination of corner orientation and permutation techniques. The Screw method uses specific screw-like sequences (repeated slash patterns) to orient and place corners. Corners are solved before edges, which simplifies the process.

Solve all eight edges using edge orientation and permutation algorithms. With corners already solved, edge algorithms are simpler because they only need to preserve corner positions. Handle edge parity if present.

pros: Simpler than Vandenbergh for many solvers
Screw patterns are intuitive and easy to remember
Fewer distinct algorithm types needed
Good intermediate method before learning full Vandenbergh
cons: Higher move count than Vandenbergh
Not widely used competitively
Limited resources compared to Vandenbergh
Less optimization potential than other methods

seven-simul.description: The 7-Simul method is an expert-level Clock solving technique used for world-record-level solves. It solves the entire Clock in just seven moves, each of which simultaneously affects clocks on both the front and back faces. Crucially, it requires no flip, meaning the solver never turns the puzzle over. This method demands exceptional mental calculation and deep understanding of Clock mechanics.

steps.description: During inspection, calculate all seven moves needed to solve the Clock. Each move must be planned to simultaneously correct clocks on both faces, and the entire solution must be computed before execution begins.

Execute exactly seven pre-planned moves. Each move involves setting pins and turning wheels to simultaneously solve clocks on both the front and back faces. No flip is performed at any point during the solve.

pros: Fastest possible Clock method — used for world records
No flip required, saving significant time
Only 7 moves for a complete solve
Simultaneous solving is maximally efficient
cons: Extremely difficult mental calculation required
Requires complete mastery of Clock mechanics and interactions
Very steep learning curve even for experienced Clock solvers
Inspection time limits can be a constraint in competition

simultaneous.description: An advanced Clock solving method where the solver manipulates multiple clock faces simultaneously by pressing specific pin combinations and turning both wheels at once. Rather than solving one clock face at a time, the simultaneous method identifies pin configurations that allow adjusting multiple faces in parallel, dramatically reducing total move count and solve time. This is the method used by top Clock speedcubers and is essential for achieving sub-4 times.

steps.description: With the Clock front-facing, analyze all 9 clock faces on the front and determine the optimal sequence of pin presses and wheel turns to solve as many front faces as possible. Use both the upper-right and upper-left wheels simultaneously when beneficial. Strategic pin pressing allows you to affect multiple clocks at once.

Flip the Clock over and solve the back faces using the same simultaneous approach. The back shares pins and gears with the front, so some adjustments affect both sides. Plan the back moves to complement the front without undoing progress.

Make any remaining adjustments to clock faces that were not fully solved during the front and back phases. This may involve targeted single-face corrections using specific pin combinations. Ideally, this step is minimal or unnecessary.

pros: Fastest Clock method — used by all top competitors
Low move count due to simultaneous adjustments
Sub-3 times achievable by elite solvers
Elegant and efficient solving approach
cons: Complex pin-combination memorization
Requires deep understanding of Clock gear mechanics
Both-sides planning is mentally demanding
Very different from other puzzle-solving approaches — no transferable skills

snyder.description: An early advanced method created by Anthony Snyder in 1982. The Snyder method starts with a 2x2x2 block, expands into a complete first two layers, then finishes with a custom last layer approach. It was ahead of its time in using blockbuilding concepts and influenced later methods. The method aims for a low move count through efficient F2L construction and specialized last layer algorithms.

steps.description: Build a 2x2x2 block in any corner of the cube. This is done intuitively by pairing a corner with its three adjacent edges and building outward.

Expand the 2x2x2 block to complete the entire first two layers. This involves solving the remaining corners and edges of the bottom two layers using a combination of blockbuilding and pair insertion techniques.

Solve the last layer using Snyder's specialized algorithm set. The method uses a unique approach to the last layer that can involve partial or full one-look solutions depending on the case recognized. Snyder developed custom algorithms optimized for move count.

pros: Very low move count (~40-50 moves)
Pioneering blockbuilding approach
Efficient F2L construction
Historically significant in method development
Teaches strong fundamental cube understanding
cons: Extremely limited modern resources
Custom last layer algorithms are not widely documented
Not competitive with modern optimized methods
Requires strong blockbuilding intuition
Almost no community support or development

soap.description: SOAP (Separate, Orient All, Permute) is an intermediate 2x2 speedsolving method. It breaks the solve into three clear phases: separating pieces into their correct layers, orienting all pieces, and then permuting them into their final positions. This structured approach makes it accessible while still being competitive.

steps.description: Separate the pieces so that each layer contains only the pieces that belong to it. Top-layer pieces go to the top and bottom-layer pieces go to the bottom, regardless of their orientation or exact position.
Orient all pieces on both layers so that the top and bottom face colors are correct. After this step, both faces should show a solid color, though pieces may not be in their correct positions.
Permute all pieces on both layers to reach the solved state. Since everything is already oriented, this reduces to a PBL (Permutation of Both Layers) step.

pros: Clear, logical three-phase structure
Relatively low algorithm count
Shares PBL algorithms with Ortega method
Good balance of speed and ease of learning
cons: Higher move count than one-look methods like CLL or EG
Three-step process means more pauses than two-step methods
Not commonly used at the highest competitive level
Separation step can be awkward for certain scrambles

ssc.description: Shadowslice Snow Columns (SSC) is a hybrid method that combines orient-first concepts with column-based solving. It was developed as an experimental approach that orients pieces early in the solve, then uses a column-building strategy to complete the first two layers before finishing with a reduced last layer. SSC blends ideas from multiple method families into a unique solving philosophy.

steps.description: Orient edges and begin constructing a partial first block on the left side. Edge orientation is prioritized early to simplify later steps and enable efficient column building.
Build columns (corner-edge pairs stacked vertically) to fill the first two layers. Columns are inserted using RU-based moves, taking advantage of the oriented edges from the first step.
Orient the last layer corners. Since edges are already oriented from the first step, only corner orientation algorithms are needed.
Permute all last layer pieces to their correct positions. With oriented pieces, this step uses PLL or can be split into corner and edge permutation.

pros: Interesting hybrid of orient-first and blockbuilding
Decent move count (~45-55 moves)
Oriented edges simplify later steps
Creative alternative to mainstream methods
Good for developing versatile solving skills
cons: Very niche with a small community
Limited tutorials and documentation
Column building requires practice to execute efficiently
Not competitive with mainstream methods for speed
Method theory is still evolving

top-first.description: A beginner-friendly Pyraminx method that solves the puzzle from the top down. The solver first orients the tips, then solves the top layer, and finally solves the bottom layer. This method is intuitive and requires very few algorithms, making it the standard starting method for Pyraminx beginners. It provides a clear, structured approach to understanding the Pyraminx.

steps.description: Twist the four trivial tips of the Pyraminx so each tip matches the color of its adjacent center. Tips can be rotated independently without affecting any other pieces, so this step is always a simple twist.

Solve the top layer (the layer around the top tip) by placing the three edge pieces into their correct positions and orientations. This is done intuitively using simple R and L moves to cycle and flip edges into place.

Solve the remaining three edge pieces on the bottom layer. This may require flipping edges or cycling them into position. A few short algorithms handle all possible cases for the bottom layer, including 3-cycles and edge flips.

pros: Very easy to learn and understand
Few algorithms required (under 5)
Good introduction to the Pyraminx
Intuitive top layer solve
cons: Not competitive for speedcubing
Bottom layer can be awkward to solve efficiently
Higher move count than L4E or keyhole approaches
Limited speed ceiling

turbo.description: TuRBo is an advanced 3BLD (3x3 blindfolded) solving method created by Erik Akkersdijk in 2007. It solves two pieces at a time using U-layer setups, providing a systematic approach that sits between beginner methods (like Old Pochmann) and expert methods (like BH/3-style). TuRBo offers faster execution than setup-based methods while requiring fewer algorithms than full commutator approaches.

steps.description: Memorize the cycle sequences for all edges and corners. The memo is structured to work with TuRBo's two-at-a-time solving approach, where pieces are paired for simultaneous solving.

Solve edge pieces two at a time by setting up target pieces to the U layer, then applying short algorithms that swap two edges simultaneously into their correct positions. This is more efficient than solving one piece at a time.
Solve corner pieces two at a time using a similar U-layer setup approach. Corners are set up and swapped in pairs using short algorithmic sequences.

pros: Faster than Old Pochmann and M2/R2 methods
Two-at-a-time solving reduces total algorithm count vs. one-at-a-time
Good stepping stone toward learning full 3-style/BH
Systematic U-layer setups are relatively easy to learn
cons: Slower execution than BH/3-style commutators
U-layer setups can be lengthy for some targets
Not commonly used at the very top competitive level
Fewer community resources compared to Old Pochmann or BH

v-first.description: A Pyraminx method that prioritizes building a V-shape (one solved edge) as the first step after tips, then solves the remaining edges. V-First is essentially the foundation shared by L4E, Bell, and other modern Pyraminx methods. As a standalone approach, V-First emphasizes flexible V selection and using intuitive or semi-algorithmic techniques to finish the last four edges. It is the standard intermediate Pyraminx framework.

steps.description: Twist all four tips to match their adjacent center colors. This trivial step can be handled at any point.

Solve one edge to create a V-shape — a solved edge with its two adjacent matching centers. The V is typically placed at the back of the Pyraminx. Color neutrality is important: choose whichever edge is easiest to place. The V can often be planned entirely during inspection.

Solve the remaining four edges. This can be done intuitively (for beginners) or with algorithms (for speed). The intuitive approach involves placing edges one at a time, while the algorithmic approach solves all four in one look. There are approximately 16 distinct cases.

pros: Natural and intuitive framework
Flexible V selection allows easy starts
Scalable from intuitive to algorithmic solving
Foundation for more advanced Pyraminx methods (L4E, Bell)
Low move count
cons: Intuitive L4E solving is slower than algorithmic approaches
Requires color neutrality for best results
Generic approach — specialized methods like Bell are faster at the top level
L4E recognition takes practice to master

vandenbergh.description: The most popular and widely-used Square-1 speedcubing method, developed by Lars Vandenbergh. The method solves the Square-1 in five distinct phases: cubeshape, corner orientation, corner permutation, edge orientation, and edge permutation. It provides a structured, systematic approach with well-documented algorithms for each step. Vandenbergh is the standard method for competitive Square-1 solving and is used by the majority of top Square-1 solvers.

steps.description: Transform the Square-1 from its scrambled shape back into a cube. The scrambled Square-1 can have pieces distributed in various non-cubic shapes between the top and bottom layers. This step involves performing slash moves (/) and layer turns to restore the cubic shape. There are many possible starting shapes, and learning efficient solutions for each is key.

Orient all eight corners so that the top and bottom face colors are on the top and bottom respectively. After cubeshape, corners may be flipped (showing side colors on top/bottom). This step uses algorithms to flip corners into the correct orientation.

Permute all eight corners into their correct positions while maintaining their orientation. After CO, the top and bottom face colors are correct but corners may be in the wrong locations. CP algorithms swap corners into place.
Orient all eight edges correctly. On Square-1, edges can be 'flipped' (showing the wrong color on top/bottom). This step flips all misoriented edges using EO algorithms.

Permute all edges into their correct positions to complete the solve. After EO, all pieces are oriented correctly and corners are placed, but edges may still need to be moved to their correct slots. EP algorithms handle the final edge arrangement, including parity cases.

pros: Most well-documented Square-1 method
Clear step-by-step structure
Used by the majority of competitive Square-1 solvers
Many resources and tutorials available
Systematic progression through each phase
cons: Five steps means more looks per solve
Total algorithm count is high across all steps
Parity handling adds complexity
Cubeshape can have many cases to learn

waterman.description: A corners-first method developed by Marc Waterman in 1981. Unlike most modern methods that build layers or blocks, Waterman solves all corners first, then uses M-slice and U moves to solve the remaining edges. It was one of the fastest methods of its era and remains a fascinating alternative approach to the cube. The method rewards strong understanding of corner permutation and edge manipulation.

steps.description: Solve all four corners of the first layer (typically the bottom layer). This can be done intuitively or with short algorithms.
Solve all four corners of the last layer while preserving the first layer corners. This involves orienting and permuting the U-layer corners.
Orient all twelve edges so they can be solved without F or B moves. This step ensures the remaining solve uses only M, U, and slice moves.
Place the four edges of the last layer into their correct positions using M and U moves.
Solve the remaining eight edges (four middle layer, four first layer) using M and U moves. Since edges are oriented, this step is efficient and ergonomic.

pros: Unique solving perspective teaches deep cube understanding
Low move count (~40-50 moves)
Ergonomic M and U slice moves in later steps
Historically significant method
cons: Very few modern resources or tutorials
Corner-first approach feels unintuitive to most solvers
Difficult to plan ahead due to non-standard order
Not competitive with modern methods for speedsolving

westlund.description: An advanced Megaminx method developed by Eric Westlund that adapts CFOP-style techniques for the Megaminx. The Westlund method uses F2L-like pair insertion for the first layers and optimized last-layer algorithms for the final face. It is essentially CFOP applied to the dodecahedral geometry, with adapted cross, F2L pairs, and last-layer algorithms. This is the method used by most top competitive Megaminx solvers to achieve fast times.

steps.description: Solve the five edges of the first face to create a star, analogous to the cross on a 3x3. The five edges must be correctly positioned and oriented, matching both the center of the first face and the adjacent centers.

Insert five corner-edge pairs into the slots around the first face, similar to F2L on a 3x3 but with five pairs instead of four. Each pair consists of a corner and its adjacent edge. Intuitive pairing and insertion techniques are used.

Continue solving the second band of faces using F2L-like pair insertions. Place the remaining edges and corners for the five faces adjacent to the already-solved first face. This extends the solve downward toward the last face.
Orient and permute the five edges of the last face to form a complete star. Use edge orientation algorithms (Mega OLL for edges) followed by edge permutation algorithms (Mega PLL for edges).

Orient and permute the five corners of the last face to complete the Megaminx. Use Mega OLL algorithms for corner orientation and Mega PLL algorithms for corner permutation. Advanced solvers may combine these steps.

pros: Fastest known approach for competitive Megaminx solving
Leverages existing 3x3 CFOP knowledge
Efficient F2L-style pairing reduces total moves
Well-optimized last layer algorithms
Used by top Megaminx competitors worldwide
cons: Requires strong CFOP fundamentals first
Many last-layer algorithms adapted for pentagonal geometry
Long solves regardless of skill level due to piece count
Complex F2L tracking across five pairs per layer

wo.description: The WO (Wedel-Odder) method is an intermediate Pyraminx speedsolving technique. It works by first solving three edges on the top layer, then solving the centers, and finishing with the last three edges (L3E). This top-down approach provides a structured and efficient solve with a manageable algorithm count.

steps.description: Solve three edges around the top tip of the Pyraminx. These edges form a ring around the top vertex and establish the orientation for the rest of the solve.
Solve the three center pieces to match their adjacent faces. The centers must be twisted to align with the already-solved top edges.
Solve the remaining three edges in the bottom layer using L3E algorithms. This is the only algorithmic step of the method.

pros: Simple and logical top-down structure
Very few algorithms required (only L3E)
Easy to plan first step in inspection
Competitive solve times for intermediate solvers
cons: Top edge solving can be awkward for some scrambles
Not as move-efficient as Nutella or advanced methods
Limited optimization potential at the highest level
Center step can disrupt lookahead flow

yau.description: The dominant 4x4 speedsolving method, developed by Robert Yau. It builds on the reduction approach but reorders the steps to allow efficient cross building and better lookahead. By solving three cross edges first, then centers, then the final cross edge alongside edge pairing, solvers can transition seamlessly into a 3x3-stage CFOP finish. Yau is used by virtually all top 4x4 competitors worldwide.

steps.description: Pair and place 3 of the 4 cross dedges (double-edges) into their correct positions on the bottom face. Leaving one cross edge unsolved preserves a free slice for efficient center building.
Build the centers for 3 of the faces used in F2L (the non-cross face centers are solved later). The free slice left by the missing cross edge makes center commutators easier.
Pair and insert the final cross edge while completing the remaining centers. This step closes the freedom of the cube and transitions into edge pairing.

Pair all remaining dedges using the 3-2-3 technique: place 3 unpaired edges in a slice, pair them with a slice move, then repeat with 2, then 3 more edges. This is the most efficient edge pairing strategy for 4x4.
With the cube now reduced to a 3x3 state and the cross already solved, complete the first two layers by pairing and inserting corner-edge pairs into their slots, exactly as in CFOP.
Orient all last layer pieces so the top face is a solid color. On 4x4, there is a chance of encountering OLL parity, which requires an additional algorithm.
Permute all last layer pieces to their correct positions. PLL parity can also occur on 4x4, requiring an additional algorithm to correct.

pros: Efficient cross construction built into the method
Excellent lookahead throughout the solve
Standard method for competitive 4x4 solving
Smooth transition from edge pairing into 3x3 stage
Very well-documented with abundant resources
cons: Center building with a free slot requires practice
3-2-3 edge pairing has a significant learning curve
OLL and PLL parity algorithms are long and disruptive
Inspection planning is difficult on 4x4

yau5.description: Yau5 is the 5x5 adaptation of the Yau method, designed for efficient speedsolving on the 5x5 Rubik's cube. It begins by solving two opposite centers, then three cross edge triplets, followed by the remaining centers, edge pairing, and finally a CFOP-based 3x3 stage. This order allows for better lookahead and more efficient edge pairing compared to standard reduction.

steps.description: Solve two opposite centers (typically white and yellow, or D and U). These serve as anchors for the rest of the solve and provide orientation reference.
Pair and solve three of the four cross dedge triplets (three-piece edge groups). These are placed into their correct positions relative to the solved centers, partially forming the 3x3 cross.
Solve the four remaining centers without disturbing the already-solved centers and cross edges. This requires careful use of commutators and slice sequences.
Pair all remaining edge triplets, including the last cross edge. This step uses slice-flip-slice and freestyle techniques to create fully paired dedges, reducing the puzzle to a 3x3 state.
Solve the now-reduced 3x3 cube using CFOP. The cross is mostly complete from earlier steps, so finish F2L, OLL, and PLL as normal.

pros: Better lookahead than standard reduction for 5x5
Early cross edges allow smoother transition to 3x3 stage
Used by many top 5x5 solvers worldwide
Natural extension of Yau4 knowledge
cons: Complex edge pairing with triplets instead of pairs
Remaining centers step can be inefficient if not practiced
Parity algorithms add time to some solves
Inspection planning is limited due to puzzle complexity

yruru.description: YruRU is a modern CP-first method created by Yash Mehta in 2020. It solves corner permutation and a line on the bottom (CP Line), then expands into a 1x2x3 block, orients edges while building the right block, and finishes with a reduced last layer. The method is designed to be particularly ergonomic for one-handed (OH) solving due to its RU-dominant moveset. It shares philosophical roots with other CP-first approaches but offers a unique step sequence.

steps.description: Solve corner permutation for all 8 corners while simultaneously placing two bottom-layer edges to form a line (similar to ZZ's EOLine but for CP). This ensures corners only need orientation later.
Extend the line into a full 1x2x3 block on the left side by adding two corners and an edge. This creates the foundation for the rest of the solve.
Orient all remaining edges while simultaneously building the right-side 1x2x3 block. Since CP is already solved, this step focuses on EO and blockbuilding using primarily R and U moves.

Solve the last layer with a reduced set of algorithms. Corner permutation and edge orientation are already resolved, so only corner orientation and edge permutation remain. This can be done with 2GLL, COLL+EPLL, or other reduced sets.

pros: Excellent for one-handed (OH) solving
RU-dominant moveset is highly ergonomic
Reduced last layer due to CP-first approach
Low move count (~42-50 moves)
Modern design with active development
cons: CP recognition has a steep learning curve
Planning CP Line in inspection is challenging
Very new method with limited proven results
Small community and few resources
Requires understanding of corner permutation theory

yu-dahyun.description: The Yu Da-Hyun method is an advanced Megaminx speedsolving technique. It follows the star, F2L, variant Westlund S2L, and last layer structure. Its key innovation is a variant of the Westlund S2L approach where the solver uses a random last layer color rather than a fixed one, allowing for more flexible and sometimes more efficient S2L solutions.

steps.description: Solve the first layer edges to form a star on the bottom face of the Megaminx. This is the standard Megaminx opening that provides the foundation for subsequent layers.
Solve the first two layers by pairing corner and edge pieces and inserting them into their correct positions around the Megaminx.

Solve the second-to-last layer using a variant of the Westlund approach. Instead of committing to a fixed last layer color, the solver selects whichever LL color results in the most efficient S2L solve. This flexibility can significantly reduce move count.
Solve the last layer using Megaminx OLL and PLL algorithms. The last layer color was chosen during S2L to optimize the overall solve.

pros: Random LL color choice can significantly reduce S2L move count
More adaptable to scramble than fixed-color methods
Builds on established Westlund S2L knowledge
Competitive at the highest level of Megaminx solving
cons: Evaluating the best LL color adds thinking time
Requires familiarity with LL algorithms for multiple top colors
Higher cognitive load during S2L decision-making
Limited documentation and community support

zb.description: An advanced extension of CFOP created by Zbigniew Zborowski and Ron van Bruchem. The ZB method modifies the last F2L pair insertion to simultaneously orient last layer edges (ZBF2L), then solves the entire last layer in one look using ZBLL. This eliminates the OLL step entirely and achieves a two-look solve after cross. While extremely algorithm-heavy (over 800 algorithms total), ZB represents the theoretical optimum for a cross-based method.

steps.description: Solve the four bottom-layer edge pieces to form a cross, identical to the CFOP cross step.
Solve three of the four corner-edge pairs in the first two layers using standard F2L techniques.

Insert the last F2L pair while simultaneously orienting all last layer edges. This step replaces the normal last pair insertion and eliminates the need for OLL. There are 306 ZBF2L algorithms covering every case.
Solve the entire last layer in one algorithm. Since all edges are already oriented from ZBF2L, only ZBLL cases apply (493 algorithms). This completes the solve in a single look.

pros: Only two looks after cross (last pair + last layer)
Very low move count (~45-50 moves)
Maximum efficiency for cross-based solving
Powerful last layer skip potential
cons: Over 800 algorithms total (306 ZBF2L + 493 ZBLL)
Enormous time investment to learn fully
ZBF2L recognition is very difficult
Very few solvers have mastered the complete set
Diminishing returns compared to standard CFOP + ZBLL

zz.description: A method that begins with edge orientation (EOLine), enabling rotationless F2L solving. Popular for one-handed solving and ZBLL finishing.

steps.description: Orient all edges and place the DF and DB edges, creating a line on the bottom. This ensures all subsequent moves are rotation-free.
Complete both first two layer blocks using only R, U, and L moves. No rotations needed since edges are already oriented.
Solve the last layer. With oriented edges, can use ZBLL (493 algs) for one-look, or OCLL+PLL for two-look.

pros: Completely rotationless after EOLine
Ergonomic R/U/L moveset
Great for one-handed (OH) solving
ZBLL finish potential
cons: EOLine is difficult to plan
Edge orientation recognition takes practice
ZBLL is 493 algorithms
Fewer resources

auf.definition: A U-layer adjustment move performed before or after an algorithm to align the last layer. Efficient AUF recognition saves time by avoiding unnecessary pauses.

algorithm.definition: A predefined sequence of moves that accomplishes a specific transformation on the cube. Algorithms are memorized and drilled for speed. Also called 'algs'.

blockbuilding.definition: A solving technique that creates blocks of solved pieces rather than solving layer by layer. Central to methods like Roux, Petrus, and FreeFOP.

corner-cutting.definition: A cube's ability to complete a turn even when the layer is slightly misaligned. Modern cubes can corner-cut up to 45 degrees or more, essential for fast turning.

cross.definition: The first step of CFOP and beginner methods. Four edge pieces are solved on one face to form a plus sign, with each edge aligned to its adjacent center color.

cfop.definition: The most popular speedcubing method. Stands for Cross, F2L (First Two Layers), OLL (Orientation of Last Layer), PLL (Permutation of Last Layer). Created by Jessica Fridrich.

cll.definition: An algorithm set that solves all last layer corners in one step, used primarily in 2x2 solving. Contains 42 algorithms.

cmll.definition: An algorithm set used in the Roux method to solve last layer corners while ignoring the M-slice edges. Contains 42 algorithms with easier recognition than COLL.

coll.definition: An algorithm set that solves last layer corners while preserving edge orientation. 42 algorithms. Used as an advanced CFOP technique to influence PLL.

color-neutral.definition: The ability to start a solve on any color cross, not just white. Doubles the chance of finding an easy cross. Most top solvers are color neutral.

cube-rotation.definition: Rotating the entire cube in your hands without turning any layers. Notated as x, y, z. Rotations slow down solves and should be minimized.

dnf.definition: A competition result when a solver fails to complete the puzzle within the time limit, or the cube is more than one move from solved when the timer stops.

dns.definition: A competition result recorded when a solver does not attempt a solve, either by choice or absence.

eg.definition: An advanced 2x2 method that solves the first face then uses a single algorithm for the rest. Split into EG-0, EG-1, and EG-2 subsets based on the first face state.

eolr.definition: An advanced Roux technique that solves edge orientation and positions the UL/UR edges simultaneously during LSE, reducing move count.

f2l.definition: The second step of CFOP. Corner-edge pairs are formed and inserted into slots to solve the first two layers simultaneously. Can be done intuitively or with algorithms (41 cases).

finger-trick.definition: A technique for executing cube moves using individual fingers rather than whole-hand movements. Essential for fast turning speed (TPS). Examples include index pushes, ring finger flicks, and thumb presses.

first-look.definition: In two-look approaches (like 2-look OLL or 2-look PLL), the first algorithm applied. For OLL, first look orients edges; for PLL, first look solves corners.

full-step.definition: A solve where every stage of the method is performed without any skips. The opposite of a 'lucky' solve. Used to indicate a solve that represents true skill.

hardware.definition: The physical cube itself and its components, including the mechanism, magnets, springs or MagLev system, and plastic type. Hardware choice affects turning feel and performance.

inspection.definition: The 15-second period before a WCA competition solve where the solver can examine the scrambled cube but not make any moves. Used to plan the cross and sometimes first F2L pair.

intuitive.definition: A solving approach based on understanding piece movements rather than memorized algorithms. Intuitive F2L is recommended before learning algorithmic F2L.

last-layer.definition: The final layer of the cube to be solved (typically the top). In CFOP this involves OLL and PLL. Advanced methods can solve it in one look (e.g., ZBLL).

lookahead.definition: The ability to track and plan upcoming pieces while executing current moves. Critical for fast F2L. Reduces pauses between pairs and is the key difference between sub-20 and sub-15 solvers.

lucky.definition: A solve where one or more steps are skipped due to the scramble. Common skips include OLL skip, PLL skip, or an already-formed F2L pair. Lucky solves are noted but still count.

magnetic.definition: A cube with magnets placed in each piece to provide tactile feedback and alignment. Virtually all modern speedcubes are magnetic. Types include edge magnets, corner magnets, and core magnets.

maglev.definition: A tensioning system using repelling magnets instead of springs. Provides a smoother, quieter feel with less friction. Standard in flagship cubes since 2022.

nxn.definition: A general term for NxNxN Rubik's Cube puzzles of any size. Common competition sizes are 2x2 through 7x7. Larger cubes use reduction methods to simplify to a 3x3 solve.

oll.definition: The third step of CFOP. All pieces on the last layer are oriented so the top face is one solid color. Full OLL has 57 algorithms; 2-look OLL uses about 10.

one-handed.definition: A WCA event where the cube must be solved using only one hand. Typically uses the left hand. ZZ is popular for OH due to its rotationless, RUL-based F2L.

ortega.definition: An intermediate 2x2 method. Steps: solve one face (ignoring permutation), orient opposite face (OLL), permute both layers simultaneously (PBL). About 12 total algorithms.

pbl.definition: The final step of the Ortega 2x2 method. Both layers are permuted simultaneously using one of about 6 algorithms.

pll.definition: The fourth and final step of CFOP. All last layer pieces are moved to their correct positions. 21 algorithms total, named after letters (T-perm, Y-perm, etc.).

plus-2.definition: A 2-second time penalty in WCA competitions when the cube is one move away from solved when the timer stops, or when inspection exceeds 15 seconds (up to 17 seconds).

roux.definition: A block-building speedcubing method created by Gilles Roux. Steps: First Block, Second Block, CMLL, LSE. Known for low move count (~48) and heavy M-slice usage.

scramble.definition: A specific sequence of moves applied to a solved cube to create a random state. In WCA competitions, scrambles are computer-generated and applied by designated scramblers.

slice-move.definition: A move that turns only the middle layer of the cube. Notated as M (between L and R), E (between U and D), and S (between F and B). Central to Roux LSE.

speedcubing.definition: The competitive hobby of solving Rubik's Cubes and similar twisty puzzles as fast as possible. Governed by the World Cube Association (WCA) for official competitions.

sub-x.definition: A milestone indicating a solver's average solve time is under X seconds. For example, 'sub-20' means consistently averaging under 20 seconds. Common milestones: sub-60, sub-30, sub-20, sub-15, sub-10.

tps.definition: A measure of turning speed, calculated as total moves divided by solve time. Top solvers achieve 8-12 TPS. Higher TPS is not always better; efficiency and lookahead matter more.

trigger.definition: A short, commonly occurring sequence of moves that appears within many algorithms. Examples: R U R' U' (sexy move), R U R' (insert), F R U R' U' F' (OLL cross). Learning triggers speeds up algorithm execution.

wca.definition: The international governing body for speedcubing competitions. Establishes regulations, validates records, and oversees official competitions worldwide. Founded in 2004.

wide-move.definition: A move that turns two layers simultaneously. Notated with a lowercase letter (r, u, l) or 'w' suffix (Rw, Uw). Equivalent to a slice move plus a rotation. Useful for F2L insertions.

x-cross.definition: Solving the cross and one F2L pair simultaneously during inspection. Saves several seconds when spotted. Double and triple X-crosses exist but are rare.

zbll.definition: A massive algorithm set (493 algorithms) that solves the entire last layer in one look when edges are already oriented. Used with ZZ method or after CFOP with oriented edges.

zz.definition: A speedcubing method created by Zbigniew Zborowski. Begins with EOLine (edge orientation + line), enabling rotationless F2L with only R, U, L moves. Popular for one-handed solving.

edge-orientation.definition: The state of an edge piece being 'good' or 'bad' relative to a reference axis. A 'bad' edge requires an F or B move to solve. EO is central to ZZ, Petrus, and Roux LSE.

corner-orientation.definition: The rotational state of a corner piece. Each corner can be oriented correctly, twisted clockwise, or twisted counter-clockwise. OLL handles corner orientation in CFOP.

permutation.definition: The positional arrangement of pieces on the cube. A piece is 'permuted' when it is in its correct location (though it may not be correctly oriented). PLL solves last layer permutation.

orientation.definition: The rotational state of a piece in its position. A piece can be in the correct position but oriented incorrectly (e.g., a twisted corner or flipped edge). OLL solves last layer orientation.

layer.definition: One horizontal or vertical slice of the cube. A 3x3 has three layers on each axis. The beginner method solves layer by layer; advanced methods break this paradigm.

center.definition: The piece at the center of each face. On a standard 3x3, centers are fixed relative to each other and define the color of each face. On larger cubes, centers must be solved.

edge.definition: A piece with exactly two colored stickers, located between two centers. A 3x3 has 12 edge pieces. Edges can be oriented (good/bad) and permuted (correct/incorrect position).

corner.definition: A piece with exactly three colored stickers, located at the vertices of the cube. A 3x3 has 8 corner pieces. Corners can be twisted in three orientations.

memo.definition: The memorization phase of blindfolded solving. Solvers memorize the position and orientation of all pieces using letter schemes and memory techniques before solving with a blindfold on.

trace.definition: The process of following piece cycles during blindfolded memorization. Solvers trace edges and corners separately, creating letter sequences that encode the entire cube state.

stackmat.definition: The official timing device used in WCA competitions. Features touch-sensitive pads that start/stop the timer. Solvers must place both hands flat on the pads to start and stop.

smart-cube.definition: A Bluetooth-enabled cube with internal sensors that track each move in real time. Connects to apps for training, solving analysis, and online competition. Popular brands: GAN, MoYu.

lube.definition: Lubricant applied to a cube's internal mechanism to modify its turning feel. Types include silicone-based (long-lasting) and water-based (faster, evaporates). Proper lubing is essential for optimal performance.

ao5.definition: An average calculated from 5 solves by dropping the best and worst times and averaging the remaining 3. The standard averaging method in WCA competitions for most events.

ao12.definition: An average calculated from 12 solves by dropping the best and worst times and averaging the remaining 10. A common benchmark for tracking personal improvement outside of competition.

bld.definition: A WCA event category where the solver memorizes the cube, dons a blindfold, and solves entirely from memory. Events include 3BLD, 4BLD, 5BLD, and Multi-Blind.

petrus.definition: A blockbuilding speedcubing method created by Lars Petrus. Steps: 2x2x2 block, expand to 2x2x3, edge orientation, complete F2L, last layer. Known for low move count and deep cube understanding.

lse.definition: The final step of the Roux method. Solves the remaining 6 edges using only M and U moves. Sub-steps: edge orientation, UL/UR placement, and final edge permutation (4c).

dual-neutral.definition: Being color neutral on two opposite colors (e.g., white and yellow). A stepping stone toward full color neutrality, offering some of the benefits with less mental overhead.

oll-skip.definition: When the last layer is already fully oriented after F2L, skipping the OLL step entirely. Occurs roughly 1 in 216 solves. Considered lucky.

pll-skip.definition: When the last layer is already fully permuted after OLL, skipping the PLL step entirely. Occurs roughly 1 in 72 solves. Combined with OLL skip, gives a last-layer skip.

two-look.definition: An approach that breaks a step into two sub-steps, each requiring recognition and an algorithm. 2-look OLL uses ~10 algs instead of 57; 2-look PLL uses ~6 instead of 21.

fmc.definition: A WCA event where solvers have 60 minutes to find the shortest possible solution to a given scramble. Typical winning solutions are 20-25 moves. Uses techniques from multiple methods.

ollcp.definition: An advanced technique that solves OLL while simultaneously permuting corners, leaving only an EPLL (edge-only PLL). Contains about 331 algorithms across all OLL cases.

wv.definition: An advanced CFOP technique applied when the last F2L pair has a connected corner-edge pair with the corner oriented. Orients the last layer corners during F2L insertion, leaving only an edge OLL.

vls.definition: An advanced technique that solves the last F2L slot and orients the last layer simultaneously. Named after Mats Valk. Contains over 200 algorithms.

x-rotation.definition: A whole-cube rotation around the R-L axis, in the direction of an R move. The cube tips forward so the U face becomes the F face. Used in notation to describe cube orientation changes.

y-rotation.definition: A whole-cube rotation around the U-D axis, in the direction of a U move. The cube turns left so the F face becomes the L face. Commonly used in F2L to change which slot you are facing.

z-rotation.definition: A whole-cube rotation around the F-B axis, in the direction of an F move. The cube tilts clockwise so the U face becomes the R face. Less commonly used than x and y rotations.

rw-move.definition: A wide move that turns the R layer and the adjacent middle layer together, equivalent to R + M' or an L move with an x rotation. Notated as Rw or lowercase r. Commonly used in F2L to avoid full cube rotations.

lw-move.definition: A wide move that turns the L layer and the adjacent middle layer together, equivalent to L + M or an R move with an x' rotation. Notated as Lw or lowercase l.

m-move.definition: A slice move turning the middle layer between L and R, in the direction of an L move. Central to Roux LSE and many last-layer algorithms. M' follows the direction of R.

s-move.definition: A slice move turning the middle layer between F and B, in the direction of an F move. Less common than M moves but appears in some algorithms and FMC solutions.

e-move.definition: A slice move turning the middle layer between U and D, in the direction of a D move. Rarely used in speed solving but appears in some edge-permutation algorithms and notation systems.

big-cube-notation.definition: Extended notation for cubes larger than 3x3. A number prefix indicates which layer from the outside to turn (2R = second R layer). A number before 'w' indicates how many layers to turn together (3Rw = three rightmost layers).

pseudo-slot.definition: An F2L technique where a pair is intentionally inserted into the wrong slot because it requires fewer moves, with the plan to correct slot arrangement later. Most useful for the first two F2L pairs when multiple slots remain empty.

keyhole.definition: A solving technique that uses an empty F2L slot as a 'keyhole' to insert edge or corner pieces that would otherwise be difficult to place. Common in beginner-to-intermediate methods and as an F2L trick.

summer-variation.definition: An advanced CFOP technique applied during the last F2L insertion when the edge is oriented but the corner is not. Solves the pair while orienting last-layer edges, leaving only corners to orient for OLL.

zbls.definition: An advanced technique that solves the last F2L pair while simultaneously orienting all last-layer edges, setting up for ZBLL or a corners-only OLL. Contains over 300 algorithms.

vhls.definition: A technique for orienting last-layer edges during the final F2L pair insertion when the pair is already formed. A subset of ZBLS containing about 32 algorithms. A practical stepping stone toward full ZBLS.

partial-edge-control.definition: The practice of monitoring and correcting last-layer edge orientation during the last one or two F2L pairs, rather than controlling edges throughout the entire F2L phase. A practical compromise that captures most benefits of full edge control.

influence.definition: The technique of choosing among multiple solutions for a current F2L pair based on how each option affects the position or orientation of future pieces. A softer form of multi-slotting that improves efficiency without additional algorithms.

multislotting.definition: An advanced F2L technique where two pairs are solved simultaneously or where one pair's insertion sets up the next pair for immediate solving. Can save 4-8 moves per solve when opportunities arise.

cutoff.definition: In a combined WCA competition round, the time a solver must beat in their first one or two solves to earn the remaining attempts. If the cutoff is not met, the solver does not complete the average.

time-limit.definition: The maximum allowed time for a single solve attempt in a WCA competition. If the solver exceeds this time, the attempt is recorded as a DNF. Typically 10 minutes for 3x3 but varies by event and competition.

combined-round.definition: A WCA competition round format where solvers must beat a cutoff time in their first one or two attempts to earn the remaining attempts needed for a full average. Used to manage time at competitions with many competitors.

delegate.definition: A WCA-appointed official who oversees competitions to ensure regulations are followed. Delegates approve competitions, verify results, and handle disputes. Each official competition must have at least one Delegate present.

scrambler.definition: A volunteer at a WCA competition responsible for applying official scramble sequences to cubes before solves. Scramblers must accurately apply computer-generated scrambles and are typically experienced cubers.

judge.definition: A volunteer at a WCA competition who administers individual solve attempts. Judges present the scrambled cube, ensure proper inspection procedure, monitor the solve, and record the time. Each solve requires a judge.

yau.definition: A 4x4 solving method created by Robert Yau. Steps: solve two opposite centers, solve three cross dedges, solve remaining centers, complete the cross, then solve as 3x3. The most popular 4x4 method among top solvers.

hoya.definition: A 4x4 solving method created by Jong-Ho Jeong. Similar to Yau but solves all centers before cross edges. Steps: solve all centers, solve cross dedges, then solve as 3x3. An alternative to Yau for 4x4 solving.

mehta.definition: A relatively new 3x3 speedcubing method created by Yash Mehta. Steps: First Block, 3QB (three-quarter belt), EOLE (edge orientation + last edge), TDR or 6CP, then finish. Designed for low move count and minimal rotations.

redux.definition: A big-cube solving strategy that reduces the puzzle to a 3x3 by first solving centers and pairing edges. The standard approach for 4x4 through 7x7. After reduction, the cube is solved using a normal 3x3 method.

vandenbergh.definition: A 4x4 and big-cube method focusing on solving edges first before centers. Less popular than Yau and Redux but offers alternative approaches. Named after Dan Vandenbergh.

cage.definition: A big-cube solving technique where a 'cage' of edges and centers is built on one side before completing the rest. An alternative to pure reduction that some solvers find more intuitive for larger cubes.

columns-first.definition: A 3x3 solving approach where the first two layers are built by constructing columns (corner-edge pairs stacked vertically) rather than using the traditional CFOP slot-based approach. A less common but valid alternative method.

etm.definition: A move counting system where every physical action counts as one move, including rotations and slice moves. Reflects the actual number of physical movements the solver makes. More relevant to real-world solving speed than other metrics.

stm.definition: A move counting system where any single-layer or slice turn counts as one move, but rotations are not counted. Slice moves like M and S count as one move. Often used in FMC discussions.

htm.definition: A move counting system where any face turn counts as one move, including 180-degree turns. R, R', and R2 each count as one move. The standard metric for FMC competitions and move count discussions.

qtm.definition: A move counting system where only 90-degree turns count as one move. A 180-degree turn (R2) counts as two moves. The strictest common metric. Rarely used in modern competition but appears in theoretical discussions.

ergonomic.definition: Describes algorithms or move sequences that are comfortable and efficient to execute with finger tricks. Ergonomic algorithms may not be the shortest in move count but are faster in practice due to better finger-trick flow.

spammy.definition: Describes a turning style or algorithm that relies on very fast, repetitive movements, often sacrificing precision for speed. Spammy algorithms use triggers like R U R' U' that can be executed almost mindlessly at high TPS.

regripless.definition: Describes an algorithm or sequence that can be executed without changing hand position on the cube. Regripless algorithms are faster because regrips cost 0.1-0.2 seconds each. A key factor in algorithm selection for speed.

rotationless.definition: Describes a solve or solving style that avoids full cube rotations. Rotationless F2L uses wide moves, back-slot insertions, and deliberate pair tracking to eliminate rotations, saving 0.3-0.5 seconds per avoided rotation.

main-cube.definition: A cuber's primary puzzle used for serious practice and competition. Choosing a main involves testing multiple cubes and selecting the one that best suits your turning style, magnet preference, and feel. Most competitive cubers have a dedicated main for each event.

setup-cube.definition: The specific configuration of a cube's adjustable features including tension, magnet strength, and lubrication. A well-dialed setup matches the solver's turning style. Solvers often spend significant time tuning their setup for optimal performance.

magnetized.definition: A cube that has had magnets added to its pieces, either from the factory or through aftermarket modification. Magnets provide alignment stability and tactile feedback. Virtually all competitive cubes since 2017 are magnetized.

uv-coating.definition: A surface treatment applied to cube plastic using ultraviolet-cured coating. UV coating changes the texture and feel of the cube, typically making it smoother or grippier depending on the formulation. Popular as an aftermarket modification.

ball-core.definition: A core mechanism design using a spherical inner core that allows pieces to glide over a rounded surface rather than sharp edges. Provides smoother turning and better corner cutting. Found in premium cubes from manufacturers like GAN and MoYu.

core-magnets.definition: Magnets placed in the cube's core mechanism in addition to the standard edge and corner magnets. Core magnets interact with piece magnets to provide adjustable magnet strength without opening the cube. A feature of many modern flagship cubes.

spring-tension.definition: The tightness of the springs (or MagLev magnets) in a cube's core, controlling how firmly the layers are held together. Tighter tension increases stability and reduces pops but slows turning. Adjusted via screws under the center caps.

ges.definition: A proprietary tensioning system by GAN that uses interchangeable colored nuts with different spring strengths instead of traditional screw-adjusted springs. Each color represents a different tension level, allowing quick and precise tension changes.

torpedoes.definition: Small plastic protrusions on cube edge pieces that extend into adjacent layers to prevent pieces from catching or popping during fast turning. A design feature that improves corner cutting and stability at the cost of slightly increased friction.

factory-lube.definition: The lubricant pre-applied to cubes during manufacturing. Factory lube quality varies by brand and model. Many speedcubers clean out factory lube and apply their own preferred lubricants for a customized feel.

cross-planning.title: Cross Planning During Inspection
cross-planning.description: How to use your 15-second inspection time to plan the entire cross before you start solving.
cross-planning.content:
The cross is the foundation of every CFOP solve, and the 15-second inspection period is your opportunity to plan it entirely in your head. Top solvers never start turning until they have a complete plan for all four cross edges. Start by identifying where each of the four cross edges currently sits, then mentally trace a solution that solves them in 8 moves or fewer. The key insight is that cross edges interact with each other, so the order in which you solve them matters enormously.

Begin your practice by solving the cross with your eyes closed. Inspect the cube, close your eyes, and execute your planned solution. If you cannot do this reliably, your planning is not thorough enough. Start with planning just 2 edges, then 3, then all 4. Many cubers hit a wall at the 3-to-4 edge transition, which is normal. Work through it by practicing 10-15 minutes of cross-only solves daily, focusing on efficiency rather than speed.

Advanced cross planning extends beyond just the four edges. Once you can consistently plan the cross in under 5 seconds of inspection, begin looking for the first F2L pair. An X-cross, where you solve the cross and one F2L pair simultaneously, can save 1-2 seconds per solve. Even if you do not solve an X-cross, knowing where your first pair will end up after the cross lets you transition into F2L without pausing.
keyPoints: Plan all 4 cross edges during the 15-second inspection before making any moves
Practice blind cross execution to verify your planning is accurate
Aim for 8 moves or fewer for every cross solution
Solve the cross on the bottom so you can spot F2L pairs immediately
After mastering basic cross planning, look for X-cross opportunities
Color neutrality doubles your chances of finding an easy cross

lookahead-fundamentals.title: Lookahead Fundamentals
lookahead-fundamentals.description: Understanding and developing lookahead, the most important skill separating intermediate and advanced solvers.
lookahead-fundamentals.content:
Lookahead is the ability to search for and track your next pieces while your hands are executing the current algorithm or insertion. It is the single most impactful skill for breaking through plateaus between sub-30 and sub-15. The core principle is simple: your eyes should always be one step ahead of your hands. While inserting an F2L pair, your eyes should already be locating the next corner and edge that will form your next pair.

The biggest mistake cubers make when developing lookahead is turning too fast. Deliberate slow solving is the most effective training method. Set a metronome or simply force yourself to solve at 50-60% of your maximum TPS. At this slower speed, you have time to scan the cube and identify the next pair. The goal is to eliminate all pauses between F2L pairs. A solve with smooth, continuous turning at 4 TPS will often be faster than a solve with 7 TPS that has pauses between every pair.

To practice effectively, try the following drill: solve F2L while narrating out loud what your next pair is before you finish inserting the current one. If you cannot name the next pair, you are not looking ahead. Another excellent drill is solving with a metronome, maintaining a constant rhythm through the entire F2L phase. Over weeks and months, gradually increase the tempo. This trains your brain to process information at turning speed rather than pausing to think.
keyPoints: Lookahead means your eyes track the next pieces while your hands execute the current step
Practice slow, pauseless solves rather than fast, choppy ones
Eliminate all pauses between F2L pairs as the primary goal
Turn at 50-60% speed during lookahead training sessions
Use metronome drills to build consistent turning rhythm
A smooth 4 TPS solve beats a paused 7 TPS solve in total time

finger-tricks-speed.title: Finger Tricks for Speed
finger-tricks-speed.description: Essential finger trick techniques that enable fast, fluid algorithm execution and higher TPS.
finger-tricks-speed.content:
Finger tricks are the mechanical foundation of fast solving. Instead of regripping the cube to make each move, individual fingers push, pull, and flick layers. The most fundamental finger tricks include: right index finger pushing the R face forward (R move), right ring finger pulling the R face back (R' move), left index finger pushing the U face away (U' move), and right index or wrist turning U. Mastering these four basic movements enables execution of the most common trigger, R U R' U', at high speed.

The key to developing fast finger tricks is starting slow and focusing on form. Each move should be executed with minimal finger travel and no wrist rotation. Watch slow-motion videos of top solvers to study their hand positioning and finger movements. The cube should barely move in your hands during an algorithm. Common mistakes include gripping the cube too tightly, using the whole hand for single-layer turns, and regripping between moves. A loose, relaxed grip allows fingers to move independently.

Once basic tricks are comfortable, work on advanced techniques: double flicks (two moves with one finger motion), table abuse (using the table surface as leverage), and pinch moves for M-slice turns. The U2 move has multiple finger trick options depending on context, including a double push with the index finger or a wrist flick. Drilling algorithms in isolation, repeating them 50-100 times in a row, builds muscle memory. Focus on one algorithm at a time until it becomes completely automatic, then move to the next.
keyPoints: Right index finger pushes for R, right ring finger pulls for R prime
Left index finger pushes for U prime, right index or wrist for U
Keep a loose, relaxed grip to allow independent finger movement
Start slow with correct form and gradually increase speed
Drill individual algorithms 50-100 times to build muscle memory
Minimize regrips and whole-hand movements during algorithms

f2l-efficiency.title: F2L Efficiency
