f2l-efficiency.description: Reducing F2L move count through smarter pair handling, fewer rotations, and better solutions.
f2l-efficiency.content:
F2L typically accounts for 50-60% of your total solve time, making it the phase with the greatest room for improvement. The average intermediate solver uses 35-40 moves for F2L, but top solvers achieve 25-30 moves through efficient pair solutions. The single biggest improvement you can make is reducing cube rotations. Each rotation costs about 0.3-0.5 seconds and disrupts lookahead. Learn to insert pairs from the back slots using R and L moves, and use wide moves (r, l) instead of rotations when possible.

Understanding F2L cases at a conceptual level, rather than memorizing all 41 algorithms, gives you flexibility. Every F2L case involves three decisions: where is the corner, where is the edge, and how are they oriented relative to each other. Learn the three fundamental situations (corner in top layer with edge in top, corner in slot with edge in top, both in top layer separated) and develop intuitive solutions for each. When you do learn algorithmic solutions, learn multiple solutions for common cases and choose based on what gives better lookahead.

Advanced F2L efficiency comes from influencing later pairs while inserting current ones. For example, inserting a pair with R U R' might accidentally displace a piece you need, while R U' R' solves the same case and leaves your next pair accessible. Multislotting, solving two pairs simultaneously, and pseudo-pairs (solving with a different slot color arrangement) are techniques that top solvers use to shave moves. Practice by doing untimed F2L-only solves, counting your moves, and trying to reduce your average below 30.
keyPoints: F2L is 50-60% of solve time and offers the biggest improvement potential
Reduce cube rotations by learning back-slot insertions with R and L moves
Use wide moves (r, l) instead of full cube rotations when possible
Understand cases conceptually rather than memorizing all 41 algorithms blindly
Learn multiple solutions for common cases to optimize for lookahead
Practice counting F2L moves and aim for under 30 moves total

color-neutrality.title: Color Neutrality
color-neutrality.description: Why and how to become color neutral, allowing you to start your solve on any of the six colors.
color-neutrality.content:
Color neutrality means being able to start your cross on any of the six faces, choosing whichever gives the easiest cross for a given scramble. Statistically, a color-neutral solver will find a cross that is 1-2 moves shorter on average compared to a single-color solver. Over thousands of solves, this adds up to significant time savings. More importantly, easier crosses lead to better first-pair visibility and smoother transitions into F2L.

The best time to become color neutral is as early as possible. If you currently average over 30 seconds, switching to CN now is strongly recommended. The transition takes 2-4 weeks of dedicated practice for most solvers. Start by adding one opposite color (if you solve white, add yellow). Practice both for a week until they feel equal. Then add two more colors (e.g., red and orange). Finally, add the remaining two. During the transition, your times will temporarily increase by 5-10 seconds. This is normal and expected.

If you already average under 20 seconds, the transition to CN is harder and the benefits are more debatable. Some elite solvers are not color neutral and still achieve world-class times. However, the general consensus in the community is that CN is worth pursuing at any level. To maintain CN once achieved, avoid falling back to a 'comfort color.' Deliberately practice your weakest colors. Some solvers use random color generators to force variety. Dual color neutrality (opposite colors like white/yellow) captures about 70% of the benefit of full CN and is a reasonable compromise.
keyPoints: Color neutrality lets you pick the easiest cross from all 6 colors each solve
On average, CN saves 1-2 cross moves per solve compared to fixed-color solving
The best time to switch is when you average over 30 seconds
Expect a temporary 5-10 second increase during the transition period
Start with opposite colors, then add pairs gradually over 2-4 weeks
Dual color neutrality (2 opposite colors) captures about 70% of full CN benefit

practice-routine.title: Practice Routine for Improvement
practice-routine.description: A structured approach to daily practice that maximizes improvement rate and avoids plateaus.
practice-routine.content:
Effective practice requires structure, not just doing hundreds of timed solves. The most efficient improvement routine balances three types of practice: targeted drilling, slow solves, and timed solves. A typical one-hour session might break down as: 15 minutes of algorithm drilling, 20 minutes of slow untimed solves focusing on lookahead or efficiency, and 25 minutes of timed solves. The ratio should shift depending on your current weakness.

Targeted drilling means isolating specific skills and practicing them in repetition. For cross, do 50 cross-only solves, timing each one. For OLL/PLL, pick your 3 slowest algorithms and execute each one 50 times. For F2L, scramble the cube, solve the cross, then practice just one F2L pair at a time, rescrambling after each insertion. These focused sessions build specific skills much faster than full solves, where each skill only gets exercised a few times per solve.

Tracking your progress is essential for staying motivated and identifying weaknesses. Use a timer app that records your times and generates statistics. Review your averages weekly, not daily, since daily fluctuations are normal. If you plateau for more than 2-3 weeks, change your practice focus. Common plateau-breakers include: learning full OLL/PLL if using 2-look, focusing on cross efficiency, drilling slow lookahead solves, or learning new F2L solutions for your worst cases. Aim for at least 30 minutes of deliberate practice daily; consistency beats volume.
keyPoints: Structure practice into drilling, slow solves, and timed solves rather than only timing
Spend 15 minutes daily on isolated algorithm drilling for your weakest algorithms
Do cross-only and F2L-only drills to build specific skills faster than full solves
Track weekly averages using a timer app to identify trends and weaknesses
If plateaued for 2-3 weeks, shift your practice focus to a different skill area
Consistent daily 30-minute practice beats occasional marathon sessions

last-layer-recognition.title: Last Layer Recognition Speed
last-layer-recognition.description: Techniques for instantly recognizing OLL and PLL cases to eliminate pauses before algorithm execution.
last-layer-recognition.content:
Recognition speed for OLL and PLL is often an overlooked bottleneck. Many solvers can execute their algorithms in under 1.5 seconds but take 1-2 seconds just to identify the case. Cutting recognition time to under 0.5 seconds effectively saves a full second per solve. For OLL, learn to recognize cases by the pattern of oriented and unoriented stickers on top, grouping similar-looking cases by their distinguishing features. The 57 OLL cases can be categorized into groups (dots, lines, crosses, corners) to speed up identification.

PLL recognition is best done using a two-sided recognition system. Instead of doing a full AUF to find a 'recognition angle,' learn to identify each PLL from any angle by looking at the patterns on two adjacent sides. For each PLL, identify the key 'blocks' (pairs of same-colored stickers) and 'headlights' (two matching stickers separated by a different color). With practice, you can recognize all 21 PLLs from any AUF angle in under 0.3 seconds.

To train recognition, use algorithm trainers that show you random OLL or PLL cases and time how quickly you can name them (without executing). Flashcard-style apps work well. Another effective method is to scramble your cube, solve to the last layer, and practice recognizing the OLL/PLL case as quickly as possible before executing. Do not time the full solve; only time the recognition pause. Over time, recognition becomes as automatic as reading words: you do not spell out each letter, you just see the pattern and know.
keyPoints: Recognition often takes 1-2 seconds but can be reduced to under 0.5 seconds with practice
Group OLL cases into families (dots, lines, crosses) for faster pattern matching
Use two-sided PLL recognition to identify cases from any AUF angle
Look for headlights and blocks as key PLL recognition features
Use flashcard apps and algorithm trainers to drill recognition separately from execution
Recognition should become automatic pattern matching, not conscious analysis

tps-vs-efficiency.title: TPS vs Efficiency: The Speed Debate
tps-vs-efficiency.description: Understanding the balance between turning fast (TPS) and using fewer, smarter moves (efficiency).
tps-vs-efficiency.content:
A common debate in the speedcubing community is whether solvers should focus on turning faster (higher TPS) or using fewer moves (efficiency). The answer is nuanced: both matter, but their relative importance changes at different skill levels. Below sub-30, improving efficiency (better cross solutions, fewer F2L moves, eliminating rotations) gives dramatically more return than turning faster. A solver doing 60 moves at 3 TPS (20 seconds) can drop to 50 moves at 3 TPS (16.7 seconds) just through efficiency, a massive improvement without turning any faster.

Above sub-15, TPS becomes increasingly important because there are diminishing returns on efficiency. World-class solvers average about 50-55 moves per solve, close to the theoretical optimum for human solving methods. At this level, the difference between a 6-second and an 8-second solve often comes down to TPS and recognition speed rather than move count. Top solvers achieve 8-12 TPS during algorithm execution and 4-6 TPS during F2L with lookahead.

The ideal approach is to train both simultaneously but with different practice types. Slow solves train efficiency and lookahead (reduce moves, improve planning). Speed drills and algorithm repetitions train TPS (faster finger tricks, smoother execution). Never sacrifice lookahead for TPS during full solves. A common trap is the 'TPS wall': solvers turn so fast they cannot look ahead, creating pauses that negate the speed gain. If your solves feel frantic and choppy, slow down until they feel smooth, then gradually speed up while maintaining that smoothness.
keyPoints: Below sub-30, efficiency improvements give more time savings than turning faster
Above sub-15, TPS becomes more important as efficiency approaches its practical limit
World-class solvers average 50-55 moves at 8-12 TPS during algorithms
Train efficiency through slow solves and TPS through algorithm drilling separately
Never sacrifice lookahead continuity for higher raw turning speed
Smooth continuous turning at moderate TPS beats fast choppy turning with pauses

competition-mindset.title: Competition Mindset
competition-mindset.description: Mental strategies for performing at your best during official WCA competitions.
competition-mindset.content:
Competition nerves are real and affect nearly every speedcuber, from beginners to world champions. The pressure of being watched, the unfamiliar environment, and the importance placed on results can cause times to increase by 2-5 seconds compared to home averages. The most important mental shift is reframing your goal: focus on the process (executing your solve well) rather than the outcome (getting a fast time). Before each solve, take a breath, clear your mind, and commit to executing your inspection plan without rushing.

Warm-up strategy matters significantly. Arrive at the competition venue early and do at least 30-50 warm-up solves before your event. Start slow and gradually build speed. Many solvers make the mistake of doing frantic speed solves right before competing, which increases anxiety and leads to sloppy execution. Instead, finish your warm-up with 5-10 smooth, controlled solves at about 90% effort. This primes your muscle memory and builds confidence without spiking your adrenaline.

During the round, treat each solve as independent. A bad solve does not predict the next one, and a great solve does not mean you should push harder on the next. If you get a DNF or a bad time, take a moment to reset mentally before the next scramble. Many personal bests happen after a terrible solve because the solver stops caring about the result and just solves naturally. Long-term, compete frequently. Competition performance improves with exposure. Your fifth competition will feel far more comfortable than your first.
keyPoints: Focus on executing your solve process well, not on achieving a specific time
Arrive early and do 30-50 warm-up solves, finishing with smooth controlled ones
Treat each solve as completely independent from previous results
Take a breath and reset mentally after any bad solve before the next attempt
Compete frequently to build comfort with the competition environment
Solve at about 90% effort rather than pushing for maximum speed under pressure

cube-maintenance.title: Cube Maintenance and Setup
cube-maintenance.description: How to properly maintain, tension, and lubricate your speedcube for optimal performance.
cube-maintenance.content:
A well-maintained cube performs dramatically better than a neglected one. Modern speedcubes require periodic cleaning and re-lubrication to maintain their optimal feel. Over time, dust, skin oils, and dried lubricant accumulate inside the mechanism, making the cube feel sluggish or scratchy. A full cleaning involves disassembling the cube, wiping down all pieces with a microfiber cloth, and reassembling before applying fresh lubricant. Most solvers should do a deep clean every 2-4 weeks depending on usage.

Lubrication strategy depends on your preferred turning feel. A typical setup involves two types of lube: a heavier silicone lube (like Traxxas 50K or Weight 5) on the core and springs/MagLev system to control speed and reduce spring noise, and a lighter lube (like DNM-37, Silk, or Lunar) on the piece contact surfaces for smoothness and speed. Apply lube sparingly; a few drops go a long way. Over-lubing makes the cube gummy and slow. Break in new lube by doing 50-100 solves after application, as the feel changes significantly during break-in.

Tensioning and magnet adjustment are equally important. Most flagship cubes allow you to adjust tension (how tight the layers are) and magnet strength independently. Looser tensions allow faster turning and better corner cutting but increase the risk of pops and overshooting. Stronger magnets provide more stability and tactile feedback but can make the cube feel sluggish. Start with the manufacturer's recommended settings and make small adjustments until you find your personal sweet spot. Keep notes on your settings so you can reproduce them after cleaning.
keyPoints: Deep clean your cube every 2-4 weeks by disassembling and wiping all pieces
Use heavy silicone lube on core and springs, light lube on piece surfaces
Apply lubricant sparingly as a few drops are sufficient and over-lubing makes the cube gummy
Break in new lube with 50-100 solves as the feel changes during initial use
Adjust tensions and magnet strength in small increments from manufacturer defaults
Keep notes on your preferred settings for consistent setup after maintenance

auf-prediction.title: AUF Prediction
auf-prediction.description: Predicting the final U-face adjustment before finishing PLL to save crucial fractions of a second.
auf-prediction.content:
AUF prediction is the practice of determining what your final U-layer adjustment will be before you even begin executing your PLL algorithm. During PLL recognition, top solvers do not just identify the PLL case and the pre-AUF needed to start the algorithm. They also note the position of a reference sticker so they can predict the post-AUF, the adjustment required after the algorithm to complete the solve. This means the very last move of the solve is already decided before PLL execution begins, eliminating the recognition pause that would otherwise occur at the end.

To develop this skill, start by learning how each PLL algorithm affects the U layer. Every PLL has a fixed AUF offset, meaning the top layer will always end up a specific number of quarter turns away from solved after execution. For example, if a T-perm leaves the U layer one quarter turn clockwise from solved, and you already know your pre-AUF, you can calculate the post-AUF instantly. Create a reference sheet for each of your 21 PLLs, noting the AUF offset for each starting angle. With practice, this calculation becomes automatic.

The real speed gain from AUF prediction is not just skipping the post-PLL pause. It also allows you to integrate the final AUF into the last moves of the PLL itself, using finger tricks to flow the AUF seamlessly into the algorithm's ending. Some solvers even choose a different pre-AUF angle specifically because it results in an easier or faster post-AUF. For instance, you might choose to start a PLL from a different angle so that the post-AUF is a U2 double flick rather than a U-prime that requires a regrip. This level of optimization is what separates sub-8 solvers from sub-10 solvers and is a hallmark of truly elite last-layer technique.

To train AUF prediction, do sets of last-layer-only solves where you pause after PLL recognition to consciously predict the post-AUF, then execute and check. Initially this will be slow and deliberate. Over weeks, the prediction becomes subconscious, happening in parallel with recognition. A useful drill is to scramble only the last layer, recognize PLL plus both AUFs, and then execute the entire last layer with zero pauses. Track your recognition-to-completion time and aim to reduce the gap between PLL start and solve completion.
keyPoints: Determine the post-AUF before beginning your PLL algorithm to eliminate the final pause
Each PLL has a fixed AUF offset that shifts the U layer by a predictable amount
Create a reference sheet noting the AUF offset for all 21 PLLs from each starting angle
Integrate the final AUF into the last moves of PLL for seamless execution
Choose pre-AUF angles that result in more finger-trick-friendly post-AUFs
Train with last-layer-only scrambles, pausing to predict post-AUF before executing

edge-control-f2l.title: Edge Control During F2L
edge-control-f2l.description: Manipulating last-layer edge orientation while solving F2L pairs to influence easier OLL cases.
edge-control-f2l.content:
Edge control is the technique of intentionally orienting the last-layer edges while inserting F2L pairs, so that when you finish F2L, all four top-layer edges are already oriented. This effectively guarantees that your OLL case will be one of only seven cases (the cross OLLs) rather than the full set of 57, dramatically simplifying the last layer. When combined with COLL knowledge, edge control can even set up one-look last-layer finishes.

The principle behind edge control is straightforward: certain F2L insertions orient last-layer edges and others misoriented them. When you insert a pair using R U R', the impact on edge orientation differs from R U' R' or R U2 R'. By learning which insertions preserve or flip edge orientation, you can choose solutions that leave edges oriented whenever possible. The simplest rule of thumb is that F and B moves (and by extension, wide f and b moves) flip edge orientation, while R, U, and L moves preserve it. If your F2L solution avoids F and B moves, edges will remain in whatever state they were in.

In practice, full edge control is difficult because it constrains your F2L solutions and can increase move count. Most solvers practice partial edge control instead: they pay attention to edge orientation during the last one or two F2L pairs and make small adjustments to ensure edges are oriented. For example, if you notice three edges are already oriented heading into your last pair, you can choose an insertion that orients the fourth. This selective approach captures most of the benefit without significantly impacting F2L efficiency or lookahead.

To learn edge control, start by understanding how to read edge orientation. An edge on the U layer is oriented if its top-face sticker matches the U-face color or the D-face color (depending on your reference system). Practice checking edge orientation after each F2L pair insertion. Over time, you will naturally notice patterns and begin choosing insertions that help rather than hurt edge orientation. Combine this with knowledge of the seven cross OLLs for maximum benefit.
keyPoints: Edge control orients LL edges during F2L so OLL reduces to just 7 cross cases
F and B moves flip edge orientation while R, U, and L moves preserve it
Full edge control on every pair is impractical; focus on the last 1-2 pairs
Check edge orientation after each F2L insertion to build awareness
Partial edge control captures most of the benefit without hurting F2L efficiency
Combine with COLL knowledge to enable one-look last-layer finishes

one-handed-basics.title: One-Handed Solving Basics
one-handed-basics.description: Getting started with OH solving, including grip techniques, finger tricks, and method adaptations.
one-handed-basics.content:
One-handed (OH) solving is a popular WCA event that challenges solvers to complete the cube using only one hand, typically the left hand for right-handed people (freeing the dominant hand for table starts). The fundamental grip for OH places the thumb on the front face, the pinky on the back face, and the three middle fingers on top. This grip allows the index and middle fingers to perform U moves, while the ring finger handles R moves. The cube rests primarily on the pinky and thumb, which provide stability.

The biggest adjustment from two-handed to one-handed solving is the limited set of comfortable moves. R and U moves are fast and natural. L moves require a table assist or a z rotation to temporarily convert them into R moves. D moves are generally avoided. This is why the ZZ method is popular for OH solving: its EOLine step eliminates the need for F and B moves during F2L, and the resulting RUL moveset is ideal for one-handed execution. CFOP solvers doing OH typically learn alternative F2L algorithms that minimize L, F, and B moves.

Finger tricks for OH differ significantly from two-handed solving. The index finger pushes for U', the middle finger pulls for U, and table abuse (pressing the cube against the table surface) becomes a legitimate technique for certain moves like D and wide moves. R2 is typically done as a wrist flick rather than a double finger push. Start by practicing OH at a very slow pace, focusing on maintaining a stable grip and clean execution. It is normal for your OH times to be roughly double your two-handed times when starting out.

To improve at OH, dedicate specific practice sessions to it rather than just doing it occasionally. Drill your OH algorithms separately from your two-handed ones, as many will be different. Common trigger sequences like R U R' U' feel entirely different one-handed and need fresh muscle memory. Stretch your fingers and wrist before and after practice, as OH solving places more strain on the hand. Most OH-specific improvement comes from refining your turning accuracy and developing the finger strength to maintain control during fast algorithms.
keyPoints: Use the standard OH grip: thumb on front, pinky on back, three fingers on top
R and U moves are fast and natural; L and F moves require workarounds
ZZ is popular for OH because its RUL moveset avoids F and B moves in F2L
Table abuse is a legitimate technique for D moves and wide moves in OH
Expect OH times to be roughly double your two-handed times initially
Dedicate specific practice sessions and learn OH-specific algorithms separately

blind-solving-intro.title: Blind Solving Introduction
blind-solving-intro.description: An introduction to blindfolded solving including memorization techniques, Old Pochmann method, and letter pairs.
blind-solving-intro.content:
Blindfolded (BLD) solving is one of the most impressive and intellectually demanding events in speedcubing. Unlike speed solving, BLD requires two distinct phases: memorization (memo) and execution. During memo, you examine the cube with your eyes and encode the entire cube state into memory. Then you don a blindfold and solve the cube entirely from memory. The timer runs during both phases. Beginners typically have memo times of 3-10 minutes and execution times of 2-5 minutes, while top solvers memo in under 10 seconds and execute in under 15 seconds.

The most beginner-friendly execution method is Old Pochmann (OP). Old Pochmann solves corners and edges separately using a single setup-move-algorithm-undo-setup-move pattern. For edges, you use a T-perm (or similar algorithm) to swap the target piece into a buffer position. For corners, you use a Y-perm or similar. The beauty of OP is that you only need to know two algorithms; all the complexity is in the setup moves that position pieces correctly before the algorithm. Setup moves are intuitive and follow simple rules once you understand the buffer-target system.

Memorization uses a letter scheme where each sticker position on the cube is assigned a letter (A through X for edges, A through X for corners). You trace the cycle of pieces starting from your buffer, noting the letter of each target. For example, if the edge memo produces the sequence D-K-R-F-M, you memorize these letters. To make long sequences memorable, solvers group letters into pairs and convert them into images or words. 'DK' might become 'Donkey Kong,' 'RF' might become 'Radio Frequency.' These vivid images are then placed along a mental journey (the method of loci) for reliable recall.

Starting BLD can feel overwhelming, but the learning curve rewards patience. Begin by learning the letter scheme and practicing tracing (memo only, no solving) until you can accurately memo a cube in under 5 minutes. Then learn the OP execution method for edges only, solving scrambles where only edges need solving. Once edges are comfortable, add corners. Your first successful blind solve is a milestone that typically takes 2-4 weeks of dedicated practice. From there, speed improvement comes from faster memo techniques and eventually graduating to more advanced execution methods like M2 for edges or 3-style commutators.
keyPoints: BLD solving has two phases: memorization (memo) and execution, both timed together
Old Pochmann uses just two algorithms with intuitive setup moves for all pieces
Each sticker position is assigned a letter; you trace cycles to generate a letter sequence
Letter pairs are converted into images or words for memorable encoding
Use the method of loci (memory palace) to store letter pair images in order
Start by learning memo only, then edges-only execution, then add corners gradually

warming-up-competition.title: Warming Up for Competition
warming-up-competition.description: How to effectively warm up before competition rounds to ensure peak performance.
warming-up-competition.content:
Warming up before a competition round is just as important for speedcubing as it is for any physical activity. Your fingers, wrists, and brain all need time to reach peak performance. A cold start in competition often results in times that are 1-3 seconds slower than your warmed-up average. The ideal warm-up period is 15-30 minutes before your event is called, consisting of a structured mix of physical preparation, algorithm drilling, and controlled solves.

Start your warm-up with gentle hand and finger stretches. Spread your fingers wide, make fists, rotate your wrists in circles, and gently pull each finger back to stretch the tendons. This increases blood flow and flexibility, reducing the risk of strain during fast solves. Next, do 5-10 minutes of slow, deliberate solves at about 60-70% speed, focusing on smooth execution and clean turning. This primes your muscle memory without building anxiety. Avoid checking your times during this phase; the goal is feel, not speed.

After slow solves, transition to algorithm drilling. Run through your full OLL and PLL sets at moderate speed, paying attention to any algorithms that feel rusty or hesitant. If you find one that does not feel automatic, drill it 10-20 times in isolation. Then do 5-10 timed solves at about 85-90% effort. These should feel controlled and confident. Resist the urge to push for personal bests during warm-up, as that increases adrenaline and tension. Your last few warm-up solves should feel easy, smooth, and automatic.

Timing your warm-up relative to your event is important. If you warm up too early and then sit idle for 30 minutes waiting for your group, your hands will cool down and you will lose the benefit. Try to finish your warm-up 5-10 minutes before you need to start competing. If there are delays, do occasional maintenance solves to keep your hands active. Also, bring your main cube and a backup to warm up with. Competition environments can be cold, noisy, and distracting, so having a familiar cube in your hands helps ground you and build confidence.
keyPoints: Begin warming up 15-30 minutes before your event with hand and finger stretches
Start with slow deliberate solves at 60-70% speed to prime muscle memory
Drill any OLL or PLL algorithms that feel rusty during warm-up
Finish warm-up with smooth controlled solves at 85-90% effort, not max speed
Time your warm-up to finish 5-10 minutes before competing to stay warm
Bring your main cube and a backup to warm up with in the competition venue

dealing-with-plateaus.title: Dealing with Plateaus
dealing-with-plateaus.description: Strategies for breaking through speed barriers when improvement seems to stall.
dealing-with-plateaus.content:
Every speedcuber hits plateaus, periods where times stop improving despite continued practice. Common plateau points are sub-60, sub-30, sub-20, sub-15, and sub-10, each representing a barrier that requires new skills or deeper mastery of existing ones. Understanding why plateaus happen is the first step to breaking through them. Most plateaus occur because the skills that got you to your current level are not the same skills needed to reach the next level. A solver who reached sub-30 by turning fast might plateau because they never developed lookahead.

The most effective plateau-breaking strategy is honest self-assessment. Record 5-10 solves on video and watch them critically. Time each phase of your solve separately: cross, F2L pairs 1-4, OLL recognition, OLL execution, PLL recognition, PLL execution. Compare your splits to benchmarks for your target average. If your cross takes 3 seconds but F2L takes 15 seconds for a sub-20 solver, F2L is clearly the bottleneck. Many solvers waste time optimizing their strengths while ignoring their weaknesses, which produces minimal improvement.

Once you identify your bottleneck, dedicate 70-80% of your practice time to that specific weakness for 2-3 weeks. If F2L efficiency is the issue, do untimed F2L-only solves and count moves. If OLL/PLL recognition is slow, use algorithm trainers. If your cross is inconsistent, do 100 cross-only solves daily. This targeted approach feels tedious compared to doing full timed solves, but it produces breakthroughs that general practice cannot. Many solvers report that after a focused training block, their times drop significantly within days of returning to full solves.

Mental plateaus are equally real. If you have been stuck at the same average for months, the frustration itself can become a barrier. Taking a 2-3 day break from cubing and returning fresh often helps. Changing your practice environment, trying a different event (like OH or 4x4), or watching competition videos for inspiration can reignite motivation. Some solvers find that switching cubes or changing their cube setup provides a psychological fresh start. Remember that improvement is not linear; it comes in bursts separated by plateaus, and every world-class solver went through exactly what you are experiencing.
keyPoints: Common plateaus occur at sub-60, sub-30, sub-20, sub-15, and sub-10 milestones
Record and review solves on video to identify your specific bottleneck phase
Time each solve phase separately to compare against benchmarks for your target average
Dedicate 70-80% of practice to your weakest area for 2-3 weeks at a time
Taking a 2-3 day break can help overcome mental plateaus and frustration
Improvement comes in bursts separated by plateaus; this pattern is normal and universal

turning-style-optimization.title: Turning Style Optimization
turning-style-optimization.description: Understanding rough versus smooth turning styles and when to use each approach.
turning-style-optimization.content:
Turning style refers to how aggressively or gently you turn the cube during a solve, and it has a profound impact on both speed and accuracy. Broadly, turning styles fall on a spectrum from rough (fast, aggressive, high-TPS movements) to smooth (controlled, flowing, moderate-TPS movements). Neither extreme is ideal; the best solvers dynamically adjust their turning style based on the phase of the solve and the specific situation.

Rough turning is characterized by fast, snappy movements with high instantaneous TPS. It is most effective during algorithm execution, where the moves are fully memorized and require no visual tracking. During a PLL algorithm, for example, you can turn as fast as your fingers allow because you know exactly what comes next. Rough turning is also appropriate for the cross, which is fully planned during inspection. The risk of rough turning is overshooting (turning past the intended position), lockups (layers catching on each other), and corner twists. Cubes set up with stronger magnets and moderate tensions help mitigate these issues.

Smooth turning is characterized by deliberate, controlled movements at a moderate pace. It is essential during F2L, where you need to look ahead and track pieces. Turning too fast during F2L creates a 'blur' effect where pieces move faster than your eyes can track, destroying lookahead and forcing pauses. The goal during F2L is continuous, unbroken movement at a pace that allows your eyes to stay one step ahead. Many top solvers describe this as the cube 'flowing' through their hands. The transition between smooth F2L and rough last-layer execution should be seamless.

To develop your optimal turning style, experiment with your cube setup. A slightly looser, faster cube suits rough turning for algorithms, while a more controlled, magnetic cube suits smooth F2L turning. Since you use the same cube for both, find a setup that balances both needs. Practice switching between styles within a single solve. Do drills where you consciously turn smoothly through F2L, then burst into maximum speed for OLL and PLL. Over time, this style-switching becomes automatic. Video-record your solves and watch for moments where your turning style does not match the situation.
keyPoints: Rough turning (fast, snappy) is best for pre-planned moves like cross and algorithms
Smooth turning (controlled, flowing) is essential during F2L for maintaining lookahead
Overshooting and lockups are the main risks of turning too aggressively
The best solvers dynamically switch between rough and smooth within a single solve
Cube setup should balance the needs of both aggressive algorithms and smooth F2L
Video-record solves to identify moments where turning style does not match the situation

multi-slotting-f2l.title: Multi-Slotting F2L
multi-slotting-f2l.description: Solving two F2L pairs simultaneously to reduce overall move count and transition time.
multi-slotting-f2l.content:
Multi-slotting is the advanced F2L technique of solving two corner-edge pairs at the same time, or solving one pair while simultaneously setting up or partially solving the next. This is distinct from simply having good lookahead; multi-slotting involves executing moves that progress two pairs within a single sequence. When executed well, multi-slotting can save 4-8 moves per solve, translating to roughly 0.5-1.5 seconds at high TPS.

The most common form of multi-slotting occurs when two F2L pairs share pieces in the U layer. Instead of solving one pair, then finding and solving the second, a skilled solver recognizes that both pairs can be influenced simultaneously. For example, when inserting a pair into the back-right slot, the moves might naturally bring the next pair's corner or edge into a favorable position. Advanced solvers plan insertions that intentionally set up the next pair. This requires seeing both pairs during inspection of the current one, which demands strong lookahead and deep F2L understanding.

True simultaneous multi-slotting, where both pairs are inserted in a single algorithm-like sequence, is rare and situational. More commonly, multi-slotting manifests as 'influencing': choosing among several solutions for the current pair based on which one leaves the next pair in the best position. This is a softer form of multi-slotting that does not require memorizing new algorithms but does require knowing multiple solutions for each F2L case and being able to evaluate their downstream effects.

To develop multi-slotting ability, start by learning 2-3 solutions for each common F2L case. During untimed solves, deliberately pause after recognizing each pair and ask yourself: where is my next pair, and which insertion for the current pair leaves the next pair in the best position? This conscious evaluation is slow at first but builds the pattern recognition needed for real-time multi-slotting. Study reconstructions of fast solvers like Max Park and Tymon Kolasiński to see multi-slotting in action. Note how their F2L pairs seem to flow into each other with minimal transition moves.
keyPoints: Multi-slotting solves two F2L pairs simultaneously or sets up the next while solving the current
Can save 4-8 moves per solve by reducing transition moves between pairs
The most practical form is influencing: choosing insertions that favor the next pair
Requires knowing multiple solutions for each F2L case to evaluate downstream effects
Practice by pausing to evaluate which insertion best positions the next pair
Study reconstructions of elite solvers to see real-world multi-slotting patterns

pseudo-slotting.title: Pseudo-Slotting Techniques
pseudo-slotting.description: Intentionally solving F2L pairs into wrong slots for better efficiency and move count.
pseudo-slotting.content:
Pseudo-slotting is the technique of intentionally inserting an F2L pair into a slot where it does not belong, planning to correct the arrangement later through a rotation or slot swap. This counterintuitive approach can save significant moves when a pair can be inserted into a 'wrong' slot in 2-3 moves but would require 8-10 moves to insert into its correct slot. The key insight is that the cost of fixing the slot arrangement later is often less than the cost of forcing correct placement now.

The simplest form of pseudo-slotting involves solving a pair into the front-right slot when it belongs in the back-right, then doing a y rotation so that the front-right becomes the back-right from your new perspective. This only works if the other slots are still unsolved, so pseudo-slotting is most useful for the first and second F2L pairs. A more advanced form involves deliberately solving two pairs in swapped positions and then using a single algorithm or short sequence to swap them into their correct slots simultaneously.

Pseudo-slotting becomes truly powerful when combined with color neutrality and flexible cross solutions. If you are color neutral, your cross can be solved in a way that positions the easiest F2L pairs near the front, allowing natural pseudo-slot insertions. Some solvers even choose suboptimal cross solutions because they enable pseudo-slots that save more moves than the cross costs. This whole-solve optimization thinking is characteristic of solvers pushing toward sub-10 averages.

To practice pseudo-slotting, do untimed F2L solves where you deliberately try inserting pairs into wrong slots whenever it seems shorter. After each insertion, evaluate whether the pseudo-slot actually saved moves compared to the direct insertion. Track your F2L move count with and without pseudo-slotting to measure the benefit. It takes time to develop the judgment for when pseudo-slotting helps versus when it creates complications, but with experience, the good opportunities become obvious. Start with the simple case of pseudo-slotting into the front-right slot and rotating, then expand to more complex scenarios.
keyPoints: Pseudo-slotting inserts pairs into wrong slots when it requires fewer moves than correct placement
Most useful for the first two F2L pairs when multiple slots are still empty
The simplest form involves inserting into the front slot and doing a y rotation
Combines powerfully with color neutrality for whole-solve move count optimization
Practice by deliberately trying wrong-slot insertions and comparing move counts
Develop judgment for when pseudo-slotting saves moves versus when it creates complications

algorithm-drilling.title: Algorithm Drilling Strategy
algorithm-drilling.description: How to learn and retain algorithms effectively through structured drilling and spaced repetition.
algorithm-drilling.content:
Learning algorithms is a fundamental part of speedcubing progression, from the initial 10 algorithms for 2-look OLL and PLL up to hundreds for advanced techniques like full OLL, PLL, and COLL. Many cubers struggle with algorithm retention because they try to learn too many at once or do not use effective memorization strategies. A structured approach to algorithm drilling makes the process faster, more reliable, and less frustrating.

The most effective method for learning a new algorithm is the isolation-repetition-integration cycle. First, isolate the algorithm: read it slowly, execute it move by move, and understand the finger tricks involved. Then repetition: execute the algorithm 30-50 times in a row until it feels automatic. Time yourself doing the algorithm 10 times consecutively and note the per-execution average. Finally, integration: practice recognizing the case on a scrambled cube and executing the algorithm in context. Many cubers skip the integration step and wonder why they freeze during solves; recognition and execution must be linked through practice.

Spaced repetition is critical for long-term retention. After initially learning an algorithm, review it the next day, then 3 days later, then a week later, then two weeks later. If you cannot execute it smoothly at any review point, reset the interval. Apps like flashcard systems can automate this scheduling. A practical approach is to learn 2-3 new algorithms per week rather than 10 at once. Quality of retention matters more than speed of acquisition. It is better to know 30 algorithms perfectly than 57 algorithms shakily.

When choosing which algorithms to learn, prioritize by frequency. Some OLL and PLL cases appear much more often than others. Learn the most common cases first to get the most benefit per algorithm learned. For PLL, the T-perm, Jb-perm, and Y-perm are among the most frequent. For OLL, cases with recognizable shapes (the cross cases, P-shapes, and W-shape) are good starting points. Also consider finger-trick friendliness: if an algorithm does not flow well with your turning style, search for an alternative. Multiple algorithms exist for every case, and the one that suits your finger tricks will always be faster than the 'optimal' one that feels awkward.
keyPoints: Use the isolation-repetition-integration cycle: learn, drill 30-50 times, then practice in context
Apply spaced repetition: review after 1 day, 3 days, 1 week, and 2 weeks
Learn 2-3 new algorithms per week for reliable retention rather than many at once
Prioritize learning the most frequently occurring cases first for maximum benefit
Link recognition and execution through practice on scrambled cubes, not just repetition
Choose algorithms that suit your finger tricks over theoretically optimal ones

cube-ergonomics.title: Cube Ergonomics and Injury Prevention
cube-ergonomics.description: Proper hand positioning and habits to avoid repetitive strain injuries from cubing.
cube-ergonomics.content:
Repetitive strain injuries (RSI) are a real concern for dedicated speedcubers who practice multiple hours daily. The repetitive, high-speed finger movements involved in cubing can lead to conditions like tendinitis, carpal tunnel syndrome, and trigger finger if proper precautions are not taken. Prevention is far easier than treatment, and good ergonomic habits established early will protect you throughout your cubing career.

The most important ergonomic principle is to maintain a relaxed grip. Many cubers, especially during timed solves, unconsciously death-grip the cube, creating tension throughout the hand, wrist, and forearm. This tension not only slows you down but dramatically increases strain on tendons and joints. Practice holding the cube with the minimum force needed to control it. Your fingers should rest lightly on the cube, using contact friction rather than squeezing force. Between solves, consciously relax your hands and shake them out.

Stretching before and after practice sessions is essential. A basic routine takes only 2-3 minutes: extend your fingers wide and hold for 10 seconds, make tight fists and hold for 10 seconds, rotate your wrists in circles both directions, gently pull each finger back to stretch the palm-side tendons, and press your palms together in a prayer position to stretch the wrists. If you practice for more than 30 minutes continuously, take a 5-minute break to stretch and rest your hands. The 30-5 rule (30 minutes practice, 5 minutes rest) is a good baseline.

If you experience pain, numbness, or tingling during cubing, stop immediately. These are warning signs that should not be ignored. Rest for at least 24-48 hours before resuming practice, and if symptoms persist, consult a doctor. Ice the affected area after sessions if you notice mild soreness. Some cubers find that switching between events (3x3, OH, big cubes) distributes the strain across different muscle groups. Using a cube with lighter magnets and smoother turning can also reduce the force needed per move. Your hands need to last a lifetime; a few days of rest is always worth preventing a chronic injury.
keyPoints: Maintain a relaxed grip using contact friction rather than squeezing force
Stretch hands and fingers for 2-3 minutes before and after each practice session
Follow the 30-5 rule: take a 5-minute break every 30 minutes of continuous practice
Stop immediately if you experience pain, numbness, or tingling during cubing
Switch between events to distribute strain across different muscle groups
Use a cube with lighter magnets and smooth turning to reduce force per move

coll-ollcp-recognition.title: COLL and OLLCP Recognition
coll-ollcp-recognition.description: Recognizing corner permutation during OLL to select algorithms that simplify the PLL step.
coll-ollcp-recognition.content:
COLL (Corners and Orientation of Last Layer) and OLLCP (OLL with Corner Permutation) are advanced last-layer techniques that solve OLL while simultaneously permuting the corners, leaving only an edge permutation (EPLL) for the final step. EPLL consists of just four cases (U-perm clockwise, U-perm counterclockwise, H-perm, Z-perm), all of which are fast and easy to recognize. This effectively upgrades your last layer from OLL + PLL to COLL/OLLCP + EPLL, which can be faster overall despite COLL algorithms sometimes being slightly longer than standard OLL.

The key to using COLL and OLLCP is recognition: you must identify both the OLL case and the corner permutation state before selecting your algorithm. During the last F2L pair insertion, trained solvers glance at the last-layer corners to determine their permutation. Corners can be in one of six permutation states: all solved, adjacent swap (front), adjacent swap (back), adjacent swap (left), adjacent swap (right), or diagonal swap. By identifying both the OLL pattern and the corner permutation, you select the specific COLL or OLLCP algorithm that solves both simultaneously.

COLL contains 42 algorithms covering the seven cross-OLL cases (where edges are already oriented) combined with the six corner permutation states. This makes COLL a natural companion to edge control during F2L. If you already practice partial edge control, learning COLL for the cross-OLL cases you encounter most frequently is a high-value investment. OLLCP extends this concept to all 57 OLL cases, totaling over 300 algorithms. Most solvers learn COLL first and selectively add OLLCP algorithms for their most common full OLL cases.

To begin learning COLL recognition, start with just one OLL case, such as the Sune (OLL 26). Learn all six COLL algorithms for that case and practice recognizing the corner permutation. A useful drill is to scramble the last layer into that OLL case repeatedly and practice identifying the corner permutation before executing. Once one case is comfortable, add another. Focus on the OLL cases you encounter most frequently for maximum return. Recognition speed improves with volume; expect to need hundreds of repetitions before corner permutation identification becomes automatic.
keyPoints: COLL and OLLCP solve OLL and corner permutation simultaneously, leaving only EPLL
EPLL has just 4 fast cases: U-perm CW, U-perm CCW, H-perm, and Z-perm
Recognition requires identifying both the OLL case and corner permutation state
COLL has 42 algorithms covering the 7 cross-OLL cases with 6 corner permutations each
Learn COLL for your most frequent OLL cases first for maximum benefit
Combine with edge control during F2L to consistently reach cross-OLL states

cross-plus-one.title: Cross+1 and X-Cross
cross-plus-one.description: Solving the cross and first F2L pair together during inspection for a faster start.
cross-plus-one.content:
Cross+1 refers to planning not only the cross but also the first F2L pair during the 15-second inspection period, so you can transition seamlessly into F2L without pausing. An X-cross is the specific case where the cross and first F2L pair are solved simultaneously in a single sequence, often with fewer total moves than solving them separately. Both techniques reduce the pause between cross and F2L and give you a head start on the most time-consuming phase of the solve.

The foundation for cross+1 is strong cross planning. You must be able to plan your cross solution quickly enough to have remaining inspection time to locate your first F2L pair. If your cross planning takes 10+ seconds, there is no time for cross+1. Work on reducing cross planning to under 5 seconds consistently, freeing up 8-10 seconds to track where the first pair will end up after the cross is executed. This tracking is the hard part: you need to mentally simulate your cross solution and predict where specific pieces will land.

A full X-cross goes further by integrating the first pair into the cross solution itself. Instead of solving four cross edges and then inserting a pair, you solve three cross edges, insert the pair (which may also position the fourth cross edge), and finish with the cross complete and one F2L slot solved. X-crosses often require creative solutions that are longer than optimal cross solutions but shorter than the combined cross + first pair. They are not always available, and forcing an X-cross when the pieces are not favorable can waste moves and inspection time.

To practice cross+1, start by doing cross-only solves but track where one specific F2L pair ends up. After executing the cross, check if the pair is where you predicted. Once your tracking is reliable, begin incorporating the pair insertion immediately after the cross with no pause. For X-crosses, use online trainers that generate scrambles with known X-cross solutions and practice finding them during inspection. Even if you only successfully execute a cross+1 or X-cross on 30-40% of solves, the time savings on those solves meaningfully improve your average.
keyPoints: Cross+1 plans both the cross and first F2L pair during the 15-second inspection
Requires reducing cross planning to under 5 seconds to free inspection time for pair tracking
X-cross integrates the first pair into the cross solution as a single combined sequence
Do not force X-crosses when pieces are unfavorable; it can waste moves and inspection time
Practice by tracking where a specific F2L pair lands after cross execution
Even succeeding on 30-40% of solves provides meaningful average time improvement

reconstruction-analysis.title: Reconstruction Analysis
reconstruction-analysis.description: How to analyze and learn from your solves through detailed move-by-move reconstruction.
reconstruction-analysis.content:
Reconstruction analysis is the process of recording your solve, writing out every move you made, and analyzing each phase for inefficiencies. This is one of the most powerful improvement tools available to speedcubers, yet it is underutilized because it requires patience and effort. A single detailed reconstruction can reveal more about your weaknesses than a hundred timed solves, because it forces you to confront exactly what you did rather than relying on vague impressions of how the solve felt.

To reconstruct a solve, you need a recording of it, either from a smart cube that logs every move automatically, or from video. Smart cubes make reconstruction trivial since they export exact move sequences. With video, you slow down the playback and write out each move. Once you have the full sequence, divide it into phases: cross, F2L pair 1, pair 2, pair 3, pair 4, OLL, PLL. Count the moves in each phase and note the time taken. Compare these numbers to your goals: an efficient cross should be under 8 moves, each F2L pair under 8-10 moves, and total F2L under 30 moves.

The real insight comes from analyzing individual decisions. For each F2L pair, ask: was there a shorter solution I missed? Did I use an unnecessary rotation? Could I have inserted from a different angle to avoid a regrip? For the last layer, check if you recognized the case from the optimal angle or wasted time with extra AUFs. Compare your solutions to optimal solutions found in online databases. You will often discover that cases you solve in 10 moves have known 6-move solutions, revealing specific areas for improvement.

Make reconstruction analysis a weekly habit rather than a daily one. Reconstruct 2-3 solves per week, choosing a mix of your best solves (to understand what went right), average solves (to find typical inefficiencies), and worst solves (to identify catastrophic errors). Over time, build a personal database of recurring inefficiencies. You might discover that you consistently use a suboptimal solution for a particular F2L case, or that you always pause at a specific point in the solve. These patterns, invisible during real-time solving, become obvious through reconstruction and give you clear targets for improvement.
keyPoints: Reconstruct solves by recording every move and dividing into phases with move counts
Smart cubes automate move logging; video requires slow-motion playback analysis
Compare your solutions to optimal ones in online databases to find inefficiencies
Analyze 2-3 solves per week: one best, one average, one worst for balanced insight
Look for recurring patterns like suboptimal F2L solutions or consistent pausing points
A single detailed reconstruction reveals more than a hundred unanalyzed timed solves

metronome-speed-building.title: Building Speed with Metronome
metronome-speed-building.description: Using rhythm and a metronome to develop consistent turning speed and eliminate pauses.
metronome-speed-building.content:
Metronome training is a borrowed technique from music practice that translates remarkably well to speedcubing. The idea is simple: set a metronome to a specific BPM (beats per minute) and execute one move per beat, maintaining perfect rhythm through your entire solve. This forces consistent turning speed, eliminates the pauses that plague most intermediate solvers, and develops the internal sense of rhythm that characterizes smooth, fast solves.

Start by determining your comfortable F2L TPS (turns per second). If you average 3 TPS during F2L, that translates to 180 BPM (beats per minute). Set your metronome to about 70% of that rate, around 126 BPM, and practice solving F2L with one move per beat. The deliberately slow pace ensures you have time to look ahead while maintaining continuous movement. Your goal is zero pauses: every beat gets a move, with no empty beats where you stop to think. If you cannot maintain the rhythm, lower the BPM until you can.

Once you can solve F2L at the slow tempo with no pauses, increase the BPM by 5-10 per session. This gradual increase trains your brain to process information at progressively higher speeds while maintaining the pause-free rhythm. Think of it like a musician increasing tempo on a difficult passage. The metronome prevents you from rushing ahead of your lookahead ability, which is the most common cause of pauses. Many solvers who try this method report that their solves feel qualitatively different after a few weeks: smoother, more continuous, and more controlled.

You can apply metronome training beyond F2L. Use it for cross execution, algorithm drilling, and even full solves. For algorithms, set the metronome to your target execution speed and practice matching the rhythm exactly. If an algorithm has 12 moves and you want to execute it in 1.5 seconds, that is 8 moves per second or 480 BPM, likely too fast for a metronome, so use the metronome at half-speed (240 BPM) with two moves per beat. Free metronome apps are widely available on phones and computers. Even 10 minutes of metronome practice per day can produce noticeable improvements in solve consistency within two weeks.
keyPoints: Set a metronome and execute one move per beat to force consistent turning rhythm
Start at 70% of your comfortable F2L TPS and gradually increase by 5-10 BPM per session
The goal is zero empty beats: maintain continuous movement with no pauses to think
Metronome prevents rushing ahead of your lookahead ability, the main cause of pauses
Apply metronome training to cross, F2L, and algorithm drilling for comprehensive benefit
Even 10 minutes of daily metronome practice produces noticeable results within two weeks

sub-60.description: Your first major milestone. This path takes you from learning the beginner method through your first efficiency improvements, getting you consistently under one minute. Focus is on understanding the cube, learning intuitive F2L, and building a solid foundation.

milestones.name: Learn the Beginner Method
milestones.description: Master the layer-by-layer beginner method. You should be able to solve the cube reliably every time without referring to notes or guides. Speed does not matter yet; consistency and understanding do.

milestones.name: Fluid Beginner Solves
milestones.description: Through repetition, become fluid with the beginner method. Reduce hesitation between steps. Start solving the cross on the bottom instead of the top.

milestones.name: Intuitive F2L Introduction
milestones.description: Begin learning intuitive F2L. Instead of solving the first layer corners and second layer edges separately, learn to pair them together and insert simultaneously. Times will temporarily increase during the transition.
skills: Recognizing F2L pairs in top layer
Handling pairs where corner is in slot

milestones.name: Sub-60 Achievement
milestones.description: Refine your intuitive F2L and begin planning the cross during inspection. Learn 2-look OLL and 2-look PLL to replace the slower beginner last layer.

sub-30.description: The bridge between beginner and intermediate speedcubing. This path solidifies your intuitive F2L, completes your PLL knowledge, sharpens your 2-look OLL, and introduces the critical skill of lookahead. Reaching sub-30 means you are a competent speedcuber ready for advanced techniques.

milestones.name: Full Intuitive F2L Mastery
milestones.description: Move beyond basic F2L pair insertion and develop comfortable solutions for every intuitive case. Reduce unnecessary rotations to one or fewer per pair by using wide moves and back-slot insertions.

milestones.name: Learn All 21 PLLs
milestones.description: Commit all 21 PLL algorithms to muscle memory. PLL is the single highest-impact algorithm set to learn because you use exactly one PLL every solve, and eliminating the second look saves 2-4 seconds immediately.

milestones.name: 2-Look OLL Efficiency
milestones.description: Optimize your 2-look OLL execution so that the two-step process is fast and seamless. Practice instant recognition of all edge orientation and corner orientation cases so there is no hesitation.

milestones.name: Lookahead Development
milestones.description: Develop the fundamental skill of lookahead: tracking the next F2L pair while executing the current one. Start by practicing slow solves at half speed with zero pauses.

sub-20.description: The benchmark that separates casual solvers from serious speedcubers. This path develops your F2L efficiency, completes your algorithm knowledge with full OLL and PLL, and introduces lookahead. Requires consistent daily practice.

milestones.name: Efficient F2L Foundation
milestones.description: Develop efficient F2L solutions for all common cases. Reduce rotations to 2 or fewer per solve. Learn to insert pairs from the back slots.

milestones.name: Full PLL
milestones.description: Learn all 21 PLL algorithms. PLL is the most impactful algorithm set because every solve ends with one, and the time difference between 2-look and 1-look PLL is 2-4 seconds.

milestones.name: Full OLL and Cross Mastery
milestones.description: Learn all 57 OLL algorithms. Master cross planning to achieve consistent sub-2 second crosses planned entirely during inspection. Begin developing basic lookahead between F2L pairs.

milestones.name: Sub-20 Achievement
milestones.description: Refine all aspects of your solve. Develop consistent lookahead to eliminate pauses between F2L pairs. Improve TPS through finger trick drilling. Aim for color neutrality.

sub-10.description: World-class territory. Sub-10 requires near-perfect efficiency, advanced algorithm knowledge beyond basic OLL/PLL, exceptional lookahead, and high TPS maintained through fluid solves. This is a long-term goal requiring months to years of dedicated practice.

milestones.name: Optimize F2L Solutions
milestones.description: Learn optimal algorithmic solutions for all 41 F2L cases. Reduce average F2L to under 30 moves. Develop strong lookahead so that pauses between pairs are under 0.3 seconds.

milestones.name: Advanced Last Layer
milestones.description: Learn COLL (42 algorithms) to influence PLL skips and reduce last layer time. Begin learning advanced OLL variations like Winter Variation and VLS for last-slot-last-layer integration.
skills: Instant OLL recognition from any angle

milestones.name: Elite Cross and F2L
milestones.description: Achieve sub-1.2 second planned crosses with frequent X-crosses. F2L should average under 5 seconds with continuous fluid turning.
skills: Advanced finger tricks and regrip elimination

milestones.name: Sub-10 Achievement
milestones.description: Push every phase to its limit. Consider learning ZBLL subsets for one-look last layer when edges are oriented. Achieve 7+ TPS sustained through entire solves.
skills: Automatic F2L with zero conscious pauses
One-look last layer for favorable cases

2x2-mastery.description: Master the 2x2 from beginner to competition-level speed. The 2x2 rewards fast recognition and short, optimized algorithms. This path takes you through the major method progressions from Ortega through full EG, building your pattern recognition and turning speed along the way.

milestones.name: Learn the Ortega Method
milestones.description: Transition from the beginner layer-by-layer approach to the Ortega method. Ortega solves the cube in three steps: build one face, build the opposite face using OLL algorithms, then solve both layers simultaneously with PBL.
skills: Fast face building from any angle

milestones.name: Full CLL Recognition
milestones.description: Learn the full CLL algorithm set, which solves the top layer corners in one step regardless of orientation and permutation. There are 42 CLL cases to learn.
skills: Prediction of CLL during face build

milestones.name: Learn EG-1
milestones.description: EG-1 extends CLL to cases where the first face is built but one piece on the bottom layer is in the wrong position. EG-1 solves both layers simultaneously from this specific setup.
skills: Choosing between CLL and EG-1 paths
Fast recognition of bottom layer state

milestones.name: EG-2 and Advanced Recognition
milestones.description: Complete the EG method by learning EG-2, which handles cases where the first face is built but with a diagonal swap on the bottom. Combined with CLL and EG-1, you now have a one-look solution for every possible state after building the first face.
skills: Prediction of EG case during face build

one-handed.description: One-handed solving is a popular WCA event that requires rethinking your fingertricks and algorithms. This path takes you from your first one-handed solve through advanced OH-specific techniques, building the dexterity and table-assisted turning that define fast OH solvers.

milestones.name: OH Grip and Basic Solving
milestones.description: Establish a comfortable one-handed grip and learn to execute basic moves with your dominant hand only. Expect your times to be 3-4x your two-handed average initially.

milestones.name: OH F2L Fluency
milestones.description: Adapt your F2L solutions for one-handed execution. Many two-handed F2L solutions use left-hand moves that are slow or awkward one-handed, so you need alternative solutions that favor R, U, and r moves.

milestones.name: OH-Friendly Algorithms
milestones.description: Replace your two-handed OLL and PLL algorithms with OH-optimized versions. OH-friendly algorithms favor R, U, and r moves, making them faster and more ergonomic.

milestones.name: Advanced OH Techniques
milestones.description: Push into advanced territory by developing OH-specific lookahead, maximizing turning speed, and refining your grip for zero-regrip solves.

blind-solving.description: Blindfolded solving is a unique challenge that tests memory and systematic execution rather than turning speed. This path takes you from understanding the concepts through your first successful blind solve and into advanced methods. Patience and consistent memo practice are more important than fast fingers here.

milestones.name: Understand Memo and Execution Concepts
milestones.description: Learn the fundamental concepts of blindfolded solving: the memo phase where you memorize the state of every piece, and the execution phase where you solve with your eyes closed.

milestones.name: Old Pochmann Method
milestones.description: Master the Old Pochmann method for both edges and corners. Old Pochmann uses a fixed buffer position and swaps one piece at a time using setup moves into a single commutator.

milestones.name: M2/OP Method Transition
milestones.description: Upgrade your edge solving to the M2 method while keeping Old Pochmann for corners (M2/OP). M2 uses M-slice moves to solve edges two at a time, cutting edge execution time roughly in half.
skills: Combining M2 edges with OP corners

milestones.name: 3-Style Introduction
milestones.description: Begin learning 3-style, the advanced commutator-based method used by top blind solvers. Instead of using setup moves into a fixed algorithm, 3-style uses direct three-move commutators to solve pieces in just 8-12 moves per target.

big-cubes.description: Expand beyond the 3x3 into the world of big cubes from 4x4 through 7x7. Big cube solving builds on your 3x3 foundation but introduces new skills like center building, edge pairing, and parity handling. This path focuses primarily on 4x4 and 5x5 proficiency with extensions to 6x6 and 7x7.

milestones.name: Learn Yau Method for 4x4
milestones.description: Learn the Yau method, the dominant speedsolving method for 4x4. Yau solves two opposite centers first, then the cross edges of one color, then the remaining four centers, then pairs all remaining edges, and finally solves the cube like a 3x3.

milestones.name: Efficient Edge Pairing and Parity
milestones.description: Optimize your 4x4 edge pairing to minimize slice moves and solve multiple edges per slice. Learn to recognize and efficiently execute both OLL parity and PLL parity algorithms.

milestones.name: 5x5 Reduction Method
milestones.description: Apply the reduction approach to 5x5, where you solve centers first, then pair edges, and finally solve as a 3x3. The 5x5 has no parity issues, making the 3x3 stage straightforward.

milestones.name: 6x6 and 7x7 Optimization
milestones.description: Extend your big cube skills to 6x6 and 7x7. These puzzles follow the same reduction framework but introduce additional center-building complexity and return the possibility of parity on even-layered cubes like 6x6.
